In treating disease, one must always examine the patient's lower condition, adjust to the pulse, and observe the patient's intentions and state of mind, as well as their illness. With those bound by superstition of ghosts and spirits, one cannot speak of the utmost virtue of medicine; with those who abhor needles and stone lancets, one cannot speak of the utmost skill. If a patient refuses treatment, the disease certainly cannot be cured, and treating it will be without effect.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Different Methods Suited to Different Regions
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When physicians treat disease, for one and the same disease the treatments differ, yet all achieve a cure — why is this? Qibo replied: This is caused by the differing configurations of the land. Thus, in the eastern region, the place where heaven and earth first give birth to life, a land of fish and salt bordering the sea, its people eat fish and crave salty food; they are content with their dwelling places and relish their food. Fish causes heat to accumulate within a person, and salt overcomes the blood; thus the people there all have dark complexions and coarse skin texture, and their diseases are mostly abscesses and ulcers. Their proper treatment is stone lancets; hence stone lancets also originated from the east.

The western region is a land of metal and jade, of sand and stone, the place where heaven and earth gather in and draw together. Its people dwell on the highlands, and there is much wind; the water and soil are hard and strong. The people there do not wear cloth but coarse felt and mats; they eat rich food and are fat. Thus evil qi cannot injure their bodily form, and their diseases arise from within; the proper treatment is toxic medicines. Hence toxic medicines also originated from the west.

The northern region is the land where heaven and earth close up and store away. The land there is elevated, and the people dwell on the highlands, with wind, cold, and ice. The people there delight in living in the open wilds and subsist on milk; cold accumulates in the viscera, giving rise to diseases of fullness and distension. The proper treatment is moxibustion with burning artemisia; hence moxibustion also originated from the north.

The southern region is where heaven and earth foster growth, the place where yang is most abundant. Its land lies low, and the water and soil are weak; it is a place where mist and dew gather. Its people crave sour food and eat fermented foods; thus the people there all have fine skin texture and reddish complexions, and their diseases are cramp and numbness. The proper treatment is fine needles; hence the nine needles also originated from the south.

The central region has land that is flat and moist, and it is where heaven and earth produce the greatest abundance of the myriad things. Its people eat a mixed diet and do not labor much; thus their diseases are mostly wilting, cold reversal, cold, and heat. The proper treatment is guided stretching and massage; hence guided stretching and massage also originated from the center. Therefore the sage combines and integrates these methods in treatment, each suited to what is appropriate, so that although the treatments differ, the diseases are all cured — this comes from grasping the true nature of the disease and understanding the overall principles of treatment.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Shifting the Essence and Transforming the Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that in treating disease in ancient times, one could cure illness merely by shifting the essence and transforming the qi, through invocation and incantation alone. In treating disease today, toxic medicines treat the interior and needles and stone lancets treat the exterior, yet sometimes the patient is cured and sometimes not — why is this? Qibo replied: In ancient times, people dwelled among the birds and beasts; they exerted themselves in movement to avoid the cold, and dwelled in the shade to avoid the heat. Within, they had no entanglements of longing and attachment; without, they had no ambition to pursue office. In this age of tranquil indifference, evil qi could not penetrate deeply. Thus toxic medicines were not needed to treat the interior, nor needles and stone to treat the exterior, and so it was sufficient to shift the essence and use incantation alone. The present age is not so: worries and troubles beset people within, toil and hardship injure their bodies without; moreover, they have lost accord with the four seasons and go against the proper measures of cold and heat. Injurious winds arrive frequently, and depleting evils come morning and night, penetrating within to the five viscera and marrow and bones, and injuring without the orifices, muscles, and skin. Hence minor illnesses inevitably become severe, and severe illnesses inevitably lead to death; therefore incantation alone can no longer cure.

Huangdi said: Good. I wish, when attending a patient, to be able to observe whether they will live or die and resolve uncertain cases; I want to know the essentials of this, as clear as sun and moon — may I hear of it? Qibo said: Complexion and pulse are what the high sovereigns valued and what former teachers transmitted. In high antiquity, the physician Jiudai Ji ordered complexion and pulse and thereby penetrated to spirit-like clarity, uniting them with metal, wood, water, fire, and earth, the four seasons, the eight winds, and the six confluences, never departing from their constancy; their transformations shift into one another, and by observing their subtlety one comes to know the essentials. If one wishes to know the essentials, then complexion and pulse are precisely this. Complexion corresponds to the sun, and the pulse corresponds to the moon; if one constantly seeks their essentials, this indeed is the essential. As for the changes and transformations of complexion, corresponding to the pulse of the four seasons — this is what the high sovereigns valued, uniting with spirit-like clarity, and by this one keeps death at a distance and draws near to life. When the way of life is thereby prolonged, this is called a sage-king. In the treatment of disease in middle antiquity, one treated as soon as illness arrived: decoctions were used for ten days to dispel diseases of the eight winds and the five kinds of numbness; if not resolved in ten days, one treated with the branches and stalks of herbs, the root and tip aiding each other; once root and branch of treatment were both attained, the evil qi would submit. The treatment of disease in recent generations is not so: treatment does not take the four seasons as its basis, one does not know the movements of sun and moon, and one does not examine whether the condition is contrary or compliant. Only after the form of the disease has already taken shape does one then wish to use fine needles to treat the exterior and decoctions to treat the interior. Crude practitioners, acting rashly and violently, believe they can attack the disease directly; thus before the original illness is resolved, a new illness arises in its place.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear the essential Way. Qibo said: The utmost essential of treatment lies in never losing sight of complexion and pulse; when one applies this without confusion, this is the great principle of treatment. If one acts contrary to compliance, and root and branch are not properly grasped, the spirit is lost and the state of the body is ruined. Discard the old and embrace the new — only then does one attain the understanding of the Realized Person. Huangdi said: I have now heard the essentials from you, Master; you have said it lies in not departing from complexion and pulse — this I now understand. Qibo said: The utmost of treatment lies in unity. Huangdi said: What is meant by unity? Qibo said: Unity is attained through grasping the root cause. Huangdi said: How is this done? Qibo said: Close the doors, shut the windows, attend closely to the patient, repeatedly inquire into their condition so as to follow their state of mind. One who grasps the patient's spirit will flourish; one who loses the patient's spirit will perish. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Decoctions and Sweet Wine Ferments
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: How does one make decoctions and sweet wine ferments from the five grains? Qibo replied: One must use rice, and cook it with rice stalks as fuel; rice grain is complete in its qi, and rice stalks are firm. Huangdi said: Why is this so? Qibo said: Rice obtains the harmony of heaven and earth and the suitability of high and low ground, and so it can reach completeness; it is harvested and cut at the proper time, and so it can reach firmness. Huangdi said: Why did the sages of high antiquity make decoctions and sweet wine ferments yet not use them? Qibo said: Since ancient times, the sages who made decoctions and sweet wine ferments did so only to have them in readiness; in high antiquity, decoctions were made but not taken. In middle antiquity, when virtue had somewhat declined, evil qi arrived from time to time, and taking them ensured complete recovery. Huangdi said: Why is it that in the present age this is not always sufficient? Qibo said: In the present age, one must use toxic medicines to attack disease within, and lancet stones, needles, and moxa to treat disease without.

Huangdi said: Why is it that sometimes the bodily form is exhausted and the blood depleted, yet no effect is achieved? Qibo said: The spirit is not being made use of. Huangdi said: What is meant by 'the spirit is not being made use of'? Qibo said: Needles and stone lancets follow the Way only when the patient's spirit cooperates. If essence and spirit do not advance, and the will and intent are not well ordered, then the disease cannot be cured. Now, when essence is ruined and spirit has departed, the nutritive and defensive qi can no longer be recovered. Why is this? Because cravings and desires are endless, and worries and troubles never cease; essence and qi become slack and ruined, the nutritive qi stagnates and the defensive qi is dispersed. Hence the spirit departs and the disease is not cured.

Huangdi said: Now, when a disease first arises, it is extremely subtle and extremely fine, and must first enter and bind in the skin. Yet skilled physicians all say: once a disease is fully formed, it is called contrary, and then needles and stone cannot treat it, nor can good medicine reach it. Now, even when skilled physicians have all grasped the method and adhere to its principles, with relatives near and far constantly heard in the patient's ear and the five colors constantly seen by the patient's eyes, and yet the disease is still not cured — how could there be any leisure left for delay? Qibo said: The disease is the root, the physician is the branch; when root and branch do not correspond, the evil qi will not submit — this is what is meant.

Huangdi said: There are cases where illness does not arise from the fine hairs of the exterior, but where the yang of the five viscera is exhausted, fluid fills the outer frame of the body, the corporeal soul dwells alone, essence is isolated within, and qi is drained without, so that the body can no longer be kept warm even with clothing. In such cases the four extremities become tense and affect the center — this is a state in which qi is repelled within while the swollen form is displayed without. How should this be treated? Qibo said: Regulate and balance it as with a steelyard, remove what is stagnant and stale, gently move the four extremities, keep the patient warmly clothed, and apply contralateral needling to the affected areas, so as to restore the bodily form. Open the ghost gate, cleanse the clean palace, let the essence be replenished in its proper time, so that the five yang qi are spread throughout, the five viscera are cleared and drained, and thus essence will regenerate of itself, the bodily form will flourish of itself, bone and flesh will support each other, and the great qi will then become balanced. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Essentials Inscribed on the Jade Tablet
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard of assessing measures and of the extraordinary and the constant, and that what they refer to differs — how are they to be applied? Qibo replied: Assessing measures means gauging the depth of a disease. The extraordinary refers to unusual diseases. Let me speak of the ultimate reckoning of the Way: the five colors, the changes of the pulse, assessing measures, and the extraordinary and the constant — the Way lies in unity. The spirit turns and does not reverse; if it reverses, it no longer turns, and thereby loses its pivot. The essentials of the ultimate reckoning are pressing and close, yet subtle; when inscribed on jade tablets, this is called uniting with the jade pivot.

When facial complexion appears in the upper, lower, left, and right regions of the face, each has its own essential meaning. If the color appears shallow, decoctions govern the treatment, and the disease will resolve within ten days. If it appears deep, potent formulas of medicine must govern the treatment, and it will resolve within twenty-one days. If it appears extremely deep, sweet wine ferments govern the treatment, and it will resolve within a hundred days. If the complexion is withered and the face gaunt, it cannot be treated, and the patient will die within a hundred days. If the pulse is short and the qi cut off, death follows; in warm disease with extreme deficiency, death follows. When complexion appears in the upper, lower, left, and right, each has its own essential meaning: appearing above indicates a contrary condition, appearing below indicates a compliant condition. In women, complexion appearing on the right is contrary and the left is compliant; in men, the left is contrary and the right is compliant. A change resulting in doubled yang leads to death, and doubled yin leads to death.

When yin and yang reverse into their opposites, treatment lies in weighing and balancing to seize back the advantage — these are matters of the extraordinary and matters of assessing measures. A pounding, struggling pulse indicates numbness and paralysis, the interaction of cold and heat. An isolated pulse indicates dissipation of qi; deficiency with diarrhea indicates loss of blood. An isolated pulse is contrary; a deficient pulse is compliant. In applying the method of the extraordinary and the constant, begin with the greater yin. To proceed toward what one cannot overcome is called contrary, and being contrary leads to death; to proceed toward what one can overcome is called compliant, and being compliant leads to survival. The overcoming relationships of the eight winds and four seasons end and begin again in cycle; if one deviates from this succession even once, it can no longer be reckoned. Thus the essentials of this discourse are complete.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Essentials of Diagnosis and the Ending of the Channels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What are the essentials of diagnosis? Qibo replied: In the first and second months, the qi of heaven is just beginning to open out and the qi of earth is just beginning to stir; the qi of the person is in the liver. In the third and fourth months, the qi of heaven is fully opened and the qi of earth is settled and active; the qi of the person is in the spleen. In the fifth and sixth months, the qi of heaven is abundant and the qi of earth rises high; the qi of the person is in the head. In the seventh and eighth months, the yin qi begins its killing work; the qi of the person is in the lung. In the ninth and tenth months, the yin qi begins to freeze and the qi of earth begins to close up; the qi of the person is in the heart. In the eleventh and twelfth months, the ice is at its height and the qi of earth is sealed shut; the qi of the person is in the kidney.

Therefore, in spring, needle the scattered transport points and the interstices of the flesh, stopping as soon as blood appears; in severe cases move the qi through, in mild cases let it merely circulate. In summer, needle the connecting transport points, stopping as soon as blood appears; when the qi is exhausted the circulation closes off, and painful conditions will certainly subside. In autumn, needle the skin, following its natural creases, applying the same method above and below, stopping when the spirit shows a change. In winter, needle the transport openings at the interstices of the flesh; in severe cases go straight down, in mild cases disperse the needling downward. Spring, summer, autumn, and winter each have their proper needling, following the method appropriate to where the qi resides.

If in spring one needles at the summer level, the pulse becomes disordered and the qi grows faint, the needling penetrates and spreads into the bone marrow, the disease cannot be cured, and it causes the person to lose all appetite and also to become short of qi. If in spring one needles at the autumn level, the sinews will contract and cramp, rebellious qi will circulate about causing coughing, the disease will not be cured, and it will cause the person to be frequently startled and to weep. If in spring one needles at the winter level, evil qi will lodge in the viscera, causing the person to become bloated; the disease will not be cured, and moreover the person will feel a constant urge to talk. If in summer one needles at the spring level, the disease will not be cured, and it will cause the person to become listless and weak. If in summer one needles at the autumn level, the disease will not be cured, and it will cause the person's heart to want to say nothing, apprehensive as though someone were about to seize him. If in summer one needles at the winter level, the disease will not be cured, and it will cause the person to be short of qi and often inclined to anger. If in autumn one needles at the spring level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to be startled and eager to do something, then rise and forget what it was. If in autumn one needles at the summer level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to favor lying down more and more, and to dream frequently. If in autumn one needles at the winter level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to shiver periodically with cold. If in winter one needles at the spring level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to want to lie down yet be unable to sleep, and when sleep does come, to have visions. If in winter one needles at the summer level, the disease will not be cured, the qi will rise, and various impediment disorders will develop. If in winter one needles at the autumn level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to be frequently thirsty.

Whenever needling the chest and abdomen, one must avoid the five viscera. If the needle strikes the heart, death occurs within a full day's cycle; if it strikes the spleen, death occurs in five days; if it strikes the kidney, death occurs in seven days; if it strikes the lung, death occurs in five days; if it strikes the diaphragm, this counts as injury to the center, and although the disease may seem to heal, within a year the patient must die. To needle while avoiding the five viscera, one must know what is contrary and what is compliant. What is called compliant refers to the true location of the diaphragm and of the spleen and kidney; one who does not know this does the opposite. When needling the chest and abdomen, one must first lay cloth padding over the area, then needle through the single layer of cloth; if the needling does not bring about a cure, needle again. In needling one must be solemn and careful; when needling a swelling, rock the needle; in needling along the channels, do not rock it — this is the way of needling.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear how the twelve channels come to their end. Qibo said: When the taiyang channel comes to its end, the eyes roll upward, the body arches backward in convulsions and spasms, the complexion turns white, and profuse sweat breaks out — once it breaks out, death follows. When the shaoyang channel comes to its end, the ears go deaf, all the joints go slack, the eyes stare fixedly as the vessel connecting to them is severed; once that connection is severed, death occurs within a day and a half, and before death the complexion first turns greenish-white. When the yangming channel comes to its end, the mouth and eyes twitch, the person is easily startled and speaks deliriously, the complexion turns yellow, the channels above and below become exuberant, numbness sets in, and then the end comes. When the shaoyin channel comes to its end, the face turns black, the teeth appear elongated and grimy, the abdomen becomes distended and blocked, and above and below cease to communicate, and then the end comes. When the taiyin channel comes to its end, the abdomen becomes so distended and blocked that breathing is impossible, the person belches and vomits repeatedly; if vomiting occurs, the qi rebels upward and the face turns red, and if it does not rebel, above and below cease to communicate, whereupon the face turns black, the skin and body hair wither, and the end comes. When the jueyin channel comes to its end, there is heat within and dryness of the throat, the person urinates frequently and the heart is vexed; in severe cases the tongue curls up and the testicles retract, and then the end comes. These are the ways in which the twelve channels fail.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Essentials and Subtleties of the Pulse
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What is the method of diagnosis? Qibo replied: The method of diagnosis should always be carried out at dawn, when the yin qi has not yet stirred and the yang qi has not yet dispersed, before food or drink has been taken, when the channels are not yet exuberant, the connecting vessels are even and regular, and the qi and blood are not yet in disorder — only then can one properly diagnose a pulse that has become abnormal. Feel the movement and stillness of the pulse, observe the clarity of the eyes, examine the five colors, note whether the five viscera show excess or insufficiency, whether the six bowels are strong or weak, and whether the body's form is flourishing or in decline; by weighing and correlating all of this, one determines the boundary between death and life.

Now the pulse is the storehouse of the blood: when it is long, the qi is well regulated; when it is short, the qi is diseased; when it is rapid, the heart is vexed; when it is large, the disease is advancing; when it is exuberant above, the qi rises high; when it is exuberant below, there is distension; when it is intermittent, the qi is failing; when it is fine, the qi is scanty; when it is rough, there is heart pain. When the pulse arrives turbid and forceful like a gushing spring, the disease is advancing and the complexion is ruined; when it departs faint and lingering as though a bowstring had snapped, death follows. The clarity of the eyes and the five colors are the outward bloom of the qi. Red should resemble white silk wrapped around cinnabar, not resemble ochre; white should resemble a goose feather, not resemble salt; green should resemble the lustrous sheen of a dark jade disc, not resemble indigo; yellow should resemble fine gauze wrapped around realgar, not resemble yellow earth; black should resemble layered lacquer, not resemble dull grey earth. When the subtle, failing form of the five colors becomes visible, the person's life will not be long. The clarity of the eyes is that by which one sees the myriad things, distinguishes white from black, and discerns short from long. If long is mistaken for short and white is mistaken for black, this shows that the essence is failing.

The viscera are the guardians of the interior. When the interior is overfull and the viscera are engorged, and the qi overwhelms the person so that he is fearful and distressed, his voice will sound as though he were speaking from inside a room — this is dampness in the internal qi. When speech is faint and the person can only speak again after a whole day has passed, this is qi that has been lost. When a person cannot keep his clothes and coverings gathered about him, and speaks indiscriminately of good and bad without regard for kin or stranger, this is derangement of the spirit and its clarity. When the granary cannot store what is given to it, this is because its gate does not hold firm. When the water spring does not stop, this is because the bladder cannot store. One who retains this guarding power lives; one who loses it dies. The five viscera are the strength of the body. The head is the storehouse of clarity and essence; when the head hangs low and the gaze sinks inward, the essence and spirit are about to be lost. The back is the storehouse of the chest; when the back is bent and the shoulders droop, that storehouse is about to collapse. The waist is the storehouse of the kidney; when a person can no longer turn and sway it, the kidney is about to be exhausted. The knees are the storehouse of the sinews; when a person can no longer bend and extend them, and walks stooped and leaning on support, the sinews are about to be exhausted. The bones are the storehouse of the marrow; when a person can no longer stand for long, and walking brings on trembling and shaking, the bones are about to be exhausted. If this strength is retained, one lives; if it is lost, one dies.

Qibo said: When the pulse runs contrary to the four seasons, an excess where there should be little is called essence-excess, and an insufficiency where there should be abundance is called dissipation. Where there should be excess but there is instead insufficiency, this too is called essence-excess; where there should be insufficiency but there is instead excess, this too is called dissipation. When yin and yang fail to correspond properly to each other, the disease is named blockage and rejection. Huangdi said: How does the pulse move together with the four seasons? How does one know where a disease is located? How does one know how a disease will change? How does one know when a disease has suddenly moved to the interior, and how does one know when it has suddenly moved to the exterior? I wish to ask about these five matters — may I hear your explanation? Qibo said: Let me speak of how the pulse turns together with the great workings of heaven. Beyond the myriad things and within the six directions, amid the changes of heaven and earth and the responses of yin and yang: the warmth of spring becomes the heat of summer, and the sternness of autumn becomes the fury of winter. The pulse rises and falls together with these four transformations: in spring it corresponds to the compass, in summer to the carpenter's square, in autumn to the balance beam, and in winter to the steelyard weight. Therefore, forty-five days after the winter solstice, yang qi begins to rise slightly and yin qi begins to descend slightly; forty-five days after the summer solstice, yin qi begins to rise slightly and yang qi begins to descend slightly. Yin and yang have their proper times, which correspond to the pulse as to a fixed schedule; when the schedule and the pulse fail to match, one can know how the pulse has diverged, and since this divergence has its own timing, one can thereby know the time of death. The subtlety lies in the pulse, and it must never go unexamined. Examining it has its guiding principles: begin from yin and yang; this beginning has its regularity, arising from the five phases; this arising has its measure, suited to the four seasons. In supplementing and draining, never lose sight of this, and be as one with heaven and earth. Grasping the sense of this oneness, one can thereby know death and life. Thus the voice corresponds to the five notes, the complexion corresponds to the five phases, and the pulse corresponds to yin and yang. From this we know: when yin is exuberant, one dreams of wading through great waters in terror; when yang is exuberant, one dreams of great fires blazing; when yin and yang are both exuberant, one dreams of killing and mutual injury. When qi is exuberant above, one dreams of flying; when exuberant below, one dreams of falling. When extremely full from eating, one dreams of giving things away; when extremely hungry, one dreams of taking things. When liver qi is exuberant, one dreams of anger; when lung qi is exuberant, one dreams of weeping. When short parasites abound, one dreams of crowds gathering; when long parasites abound, one dreams of fighting and mutual injury. Therefore there is a proper way to hold the pulse: emptiness and stillness of mind are what preserve its accuracy. In spring the pulse floats, like a fish swimming beneath the waves; in summer it lies at the skin, brimming over as though the myriad things had abundance; in autumn it sinks below the skin, like hibernating insects about to withdraw; in winter it lies at the bone, like hibernating insects sealed tightly away, while the noble person stays indoors. Hence it is said: one who understands the interior presses the pulse and takes careful note of it; one who understands the exterior follows it from end back to beginning. These six points are the great rules for holding the pulse.

When the heart pulse beats hard and long, the disease should manifest as a curled tongue with inability to speak; when it is soft and scattered, the disease should resolve of itself within a cycle. When the lung pulse beats hard and long, the disease should manifest as spitting blood; when it is soft and scattered, the disease should manifest as profuse sweating that even now will not stop recurring. When the liver pulse beats hard and long, and the complexion is not green, the disease should manifest as though from a fall or a blow, with blood collecting below the ribs and causing the person to pant with rebellious qi; when it is soft, scattered, and of lustrous color, the disease should be overflow-drink, in which the person becomes suddenly very thirsty and drinks excessively, so that the fluid easily penetrates the muscles, skin, and the regions beyond the intestines and stomach. When the stomach pulse beats hard and long, and the complexion is red, the disease should manifest as a fracture of the thigh; when it is soft and scattered, the disease should manifest as food impediment. When the spleen pulse beats hard and long, and the complexion is yellow, the disease should manifest as shortness of qi; when it is soft, scattered, and dull in color, the disease should manifest as swelling of the lower leg, resembling a condition of water retention. When the kidney pulse beats hard and long, and the complexion is yellow and red, the disease should manifest as a wrenched lower back; when it is soft and scattered, the disease should manifest as scantiness of blood that even now will not be restored. Huangdi said: When in diagnosis one finds the heart pulse tense, what disease is this, and what does it look like? Qibo said: The disease is called heart mounting; there should be a palpable mass in the lower abdomen. Huangdi said: On what grounds do you say this? Qibo said: The heart is a yang viscus, and the small intestine serves as its envoy; hence I say there should be a palpable mass in the lower abdomen. Huangdi said: When in diagnosis one finds the stomach pulse, what does the disease look like? Qibo said: When the stomach pulse is in excess, there is distension; when it is deficient, there is diarrhea.

Huangdi said: What is meant by a disease forming and then transforming? Qibo said: Wind disorder, once formed, becomes cold-heat; heat-jaundice, once formed, becomes wasting-thirst; reversal, once formed, becomes disease of the vertex; prolonged wind becomes diarrhea with undigested food; wind lodged in the vessels becomes a severe skin disease. The transformations of disease are too numerous to count. Huangdi said: All the various swellings and abscesses, contractures of the sinews, and pains in the bones — from what do these arise? Qibo said: These are swellings caused by cold qi, transformations brought about by the eight winds. Huangdi said: How does one treat them? Qibo said: These are diseases of the four seasons; treat them using what overcomes the offending qi, and they will be cured. Huangdi said: When an old disease stirs activity within the five viscera and thereby injures the pulse and complexion, how in each case can one know whether the disease is long-standing or has arrived suddenly? Qibo said: How thorough is this question! When one finds the pulse diminished but the complexion not lost, this is a new disease. When one finds the pulse not lost but the complexion lost, this is a long-standing disease. When one finds both the pulse and the five colors lost, this too is a long-standing disease. When one finds neither the pulse nor the five colors lost, this is a new disease. When the liver pulse and kidney pulse arrive together, and the complexion is greenish-red, the disease should stem from injury; if no blood has yet appeared, then once blood does appear, it will be as damp as though soaked in water.

Within both sides of the cubit position lie the free ribs; the outer aspect of the cubit position reflects the kidney, the inner aspect reflects the abdomen. Just above this, on the left: the outer aspect reflects the liver, the inner aspect reflects the diaphragm; on the right: the outer aspect reflects the stomach, the inner aspect reflects the spleen. Above this again, on the right: the outer aspect reflects the lung, the inner aspect reflects the chest; on the left: the outer aspect reflects the heart, the inner aspect reflects the membrane surrounding the heart. The front reflects the front of the body, the back reflects the back of the body. What is uppermost, at the very top, pertains to matters of the chest and throat; what is lowermost, at the very bottom, pertains to matters of the lower abdomen, lower back, thighs, knees, shins, and feet.

When the pulse is coarse and large, this is insufficiency of yin and excess of yang, producing heat within. When it comes rapidly and departs slowly, replete above and empty below, this is reversal disease of the vertex; when it comes slowly and departs rapidly, empty above and replete below, this is aversion to wind. Hence when a person is struck by pathogenic wind, it is the yang qi that receives it. When the pulse is sunken, fine, and rapid throughout, this is shaoyin reversal; when it is sunken, fine, rapid, and scattered, this is cold-heat disorder; when it is floating and scattered, this causes dizziness and collapse. All pulses that are floating without agitation belong to yang and indicate heat; those that are also agitated indicate disease in the hands. All pulses that are fine and sunken belong to yin and indicate bone pain; those that are also still indicate disease in the feet. A pulse that beats rapidly with one intermittent beat indicates disease in the yang channels, with diarrhea and stool containing pus and blood. In all abnormal cases, upon palpation: a rough pulse indicates excess of yang qi, a slippery pulse indicates excess of yin qi. Excess of yang qi produces fever of the body without sweating; excess of yin qi produces profuse sweating with a cold body; excess of both yin and yang together produces cold without sweating. When one presses outward and the pulse is felt inside but not outside, there is accumulation in the heart and abdomen. When one presses inward and the pulse is felt outside but not inside, there is heat in the body. When one presses upward and the pulse is felt above but not below, the lower back and feet are cold. When one presses downward and the pulse is felt below but not above, there is pain in the head and neck. When one presses down to the bone and the pulse qi is scanty, there is pain in the lower back and spine, together with impediment in the body.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Qi Manifestations of the Healthy Person
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What is the pulse of a healthy person like? Qibo replied: In a person, with one exhalation the pulse beats twice, and with one inhalation the pulse also beats twice; over a complete breath, counting the pause between breaths, the pulse beats five times — this is called a healthy person. A healthy person is one who is free of disease. One should always use a person free of disease to regulate the diagnosis of a sick person; since the physician himself is not ill, he uses his own settled breathing to regulate the count for the patient — this is the rule. When a person's pulse beats only once with each exhalation and once with each inhalation, this is called insufficiency of qi. When a person's pulse beats three times with each exhalation and three times with each inhalation and is agitated, with heat at the cubit position, this is called warm disease; if the cubit is not hot but the pulse is slippery, this is called wind disease; if the pulse is rough, this is called impediment. When a person's pulse beats four times or more with each exhalation, this indicates death; when the pulse is cut off and fails to arrive, this indicates death; when the pulse is at times sparse and at times rapid, this indicates death.

The constant qi of a healthy person is endowed by the stomach; the stomach is the constant qi of a healthy person. When a person lacks stomach qi, this is called rebellion, and one in rebellion dies. In spring, when the stomach pulse is slightly wiry, this is called healthy; when it is very wiry with little stomach qi, this is called liver disease; when it is purely wiry with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when there is stomach qi together with a hair-like floating quality, this indicates a disease due in autumn, and if the hair-like quality is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus is dispersed through the liver, which stores the qi of the sinews and membranes. In summer, when the stomach pulse is slightly hook-like, this is called healthy; when it is very hook-like with little stomach qi, this is called heart disease; when it is purely hook-like with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when there is stomach qi together with a stone-like sunken quality, this indicates a disease due in winter, and if the stone-like quality is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus penetrates through the heart, which stores the qi of the blood and vessels. In late summer, when the stomach pulse is slightly soft and weak, this is called healthy; when it is very weak with little stomach qi, this is called spleen disease; when it is purely intermittent with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when soft and weak together with a stone-like quality, this indicates a disease due in winter, and if the weakness is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus moistens the spleen, which stores the qi of the muscles and flesh. In autumn, when the stomach pulse is slightly hair-like, this is called healthy; when it is very hair-like with little stomach qi, this is called lung disease; when it is purely hair-like with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when hair-like together with a wiry quality, this indicates a disease due in spring, and if the wiry quality is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus rises up to the lung, by which it circulates the nutritive and defensive qi, and yin and yang. In winter, when the stomach pulse is slightly stone-like, this is called healthy; when it is very stone-like with little stomach qi, this is called kidney disease; when it is purely stone-like with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when stone-like together with a hook-like quality, this indicates a disease due in summer, and if the hook-like quality is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus descends to the kidney, which stores the qi of the bones and marrow. The great connecting vessel of the stomach is called Xu Li; it passes through the diaphragm and connects with the lung, emerging below the left breast, where its beating can be felt through the clothing — this is the pulse of the ancestral qi. If it is exuberant, panting, and frequently intermittent, the disease is in the interior; if it is knotted and moves crosswise, there is an accumulation; if it is cut off and fails to arrive, this indicates death. When the beating below the breast can be felt through the clothing, this shows that the ancestral qi is leaking away.

To know the excess and insufficiency of the cun kou pulse at the wrist: when the cun kou pulse felt beneath the hand is short, this indicates headache; when it is long, this indicates pain in the feet and shins; when it is hurried and strikes upward, this indicates pain in the shoulders and back. When the cun kou pulse is sunken and hard, this indicates disease in the interior; when it is floating and exuberant, this indicates disease on the exterior. When it is sunken and weak, this indicates cold-heat together with abdominal masses and pain in the lower abdomen. When it is sunken and moves crosswise, this indicates accumulation below the ribs, with pain from a crosswise accumulation in the abdomen. When it is sunken and racing, this indicates cold-heat. When the pulse is exuberant, slippery, and hard, this indicates disease on the exterior; when it is small, replete, and hard, the disease is in the interior. When the pulse is small, weak, and rough, this is called a long-standing disease; when it is slippery, floating, and rapid, this is called a new disease. When the pulse is tense, this indicates abdominal masses with pain in the lower abdomen. A slippery pulse indicates wind; a rough pulse indicates impediment; a moderate and slippery pulse indicates heat in the interior; an exuberant and tight pulse indicates distension.

When the pulse follows yin and yang appropriately, the disease is easy to cure; when the pulse runs contrary to yin and yang, the disease is difficult to cure. When the pulse accords with the proper sequence of the four seasons, it is said that the disease presents no complications; when the pulse runs contrary to the four seasons, or fails to correspond well to its governing viscus, it is said to be difficult to cure.

When there are many bluish-green vessels visible on the arm, this indicates loss of blood. When the cubit pulse is moderate and rough, this is called the languor-and-weakness syndrome. When a person lies quietly at ease yet the pulse is exuberant, this indicates loss of blood. When the cubit skin is rough but the pulse is slippery, this indicates excessive sweating. When the cubit skin is cold and the pulse is fine, this indicates diarrhea. When the cubit skin is coarse and constantly hot, this indicates heat in the interior.

When a diseased liver pulse appears on a gengxin day, death follows; when a diseased heart pulse appears on a rengui day, death follows; when a diseased spleen pulse appears on a jiayi day, death follows; when a diseased lung pulse appears on a bingding day, death follows; when a diseased kidney pulse appears on a wuji day, death follows. This is what is meant by saying that whenever the true, unmediated visceral pulse appears without any admixture of stomach qi, death always follows.

When the vessels of the neck pulsate visibly together with panting and rapid coughing, this indicates water disease. When the area beneath the eyes is slightly swollen, resembling a reclining silkworm, this indicates water disease. When the urine is yellow-red and the person prefers to lie quietly, this is jaundice. When a person feels hungry again immediately after eating, this is stomach jaundice. Swelling of the face indicates wind. Swelling of the feet and shins indicates water disease. Yellowing of the eyes indicates jaundice.
In a woman, when the shaoyin pulse of the hand beats vigorously, this indicates pregnancy.

The pulse may be contrary to or compliant with the four seasons even before any visceral pattern has appeared: if in spring or summer the pulse is thin, or if in autumn or winter the pulse is floating and large, this is called being contrary to the four seasons. When there is wind-heat yet the pulse is quiet, when there is diarrhea or blood loss yet the pulse is replete, when the disease is in the interior yet the pulse is deficient, when the disease is on the exterior yet the pulse is rough and hard — in all these cases the disease is difficult to treat; this too is called running contrary to the four seasons.

A person takes water and grain as the root of life; therefore if a person is cut off from water and grain, death follows, and if the pulse lacks stomach qi, death also follows. What is meant by lacking stomach qi is that only the true, unmediated visceral pulse is obtained, without any stomach qi present. What is meant by the pulse failing to obtain stomach qi is, for instance, a liver pulse with no wiry quality at all, or a kidney pulse with no stone-like quality at all. When the taiyang pulse arrives, it is surging, large, and long. When the shaoyang pulse arrives, it is at times rapid and at times sparse, at times short and at times long. When the yangming pulse arrives, it is floating, large, and short. Now, the healthy heart pulse comes strung together bead upon bead, like fingering a string of fine jade beads — this is called a healthy heart pulse; in summer, stomach qi is its root. The diseased heart pulse comes panting and continuous, slightly curved in its course — this is called heart disease. The dying heart pulse comes curved in front and stationary behind, like grasping a curved hook — this is called death of the heart. The healthy lung pulse comes languid and fluttering, like elm pods falling — this is called a healthy lung pulse; in autumn, stomach qi is its root. The diseased lung pulse comes neither rising nor falling, like stroking a chicken's feather — this is called lung disease. The dying lung pulse comes like a floating object, like wind blowing through hair — this is called death of the lung. The healthy liver pulse comes soft, weak, and swaying, like raising the tip of a long pole — this is called a healthy liver pulse; in spring, stomach qi is its root. The diseased liver pulse comes full, replete, and slippery, like stroking a long pole — this is called liver disease. The dying liver pulse comes tense and increasingly taut, like a newly strung bowstring — this is called death of the liver. The healthy spleen pulse comes harmonious and gentle, with its beats set apart, like a hen treading the ground — this is called a healthy spleen pulse; in late summer, stomach qi is its root. The diseased spleen pulse comes replete and rapidly full, like a hen lifting its foot — this is called spleen disease. The dying spleen pulse comes sharp and hard like a crow's beak, like a bird's spur, like a leaking roof, like flowing water — this is called death of the spleen. The healthy kidney pulse comes panting and strung together like a hook, firm when pressed — this is called a healthy kidney pulse; in winter, stomach qi is its root. The diseased kidney pulse comes like pulling on a vine, growing firmer under pressure — this is called kidney disease. The dying kidney pulse comes bursting forth like a snapped rope, striking abruptly like a flicked stone — this is called death of the kidney.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Jade Mechanism and the True Viscera
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: The spring pulse is like a bowstring — in what way is it like a bowstring? Qibo replied: The spring pulse corresponds to the liver, to wood of the east, the direction by which the myriad things begin to be born. Therefore its qi comes soft and weak, light and empty, yet smooth, straight and upright, and long — hence it is called "bowstring-like." Anything contrary to this is disease. Huangdi said: What is meant by contrary to this? Qibo said: If the qi comes full and forceful, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if the qi comes insubstantial and faint, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: In excess and insufficiency of the spring pulse, what are the diseases like in each case? Qibo said: In excess, it makes a person forgetful, dazed and dizzy, with disorders of the crown of the head; in insufficiency, it makes the chest ache, drawing into the back, and below causes fullness in both flanks and the sides beneath the ribs. Huangdi said: Good.

The summer pulse is like a hook — in what way is it like a hook? Qibo said: The summer pulse corresponds to the heart, to fire of the south, the direction by which the myriad things flourish and grow. Therefore its qi comes abundant and departs in decline — hence it is called "hook-like." Anything contrary to this is disease. Huangdi said: What is meant by contrary to this? Qibo said: If the qi comes abundant and also departs abundant, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if the qi comes not abundant but departs abundant instead, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: In excess and insufficiency of the summer pulse, what are the diseases like in each case? Qibo said: In excess, it makes the body hot and the skin ache, producing spreading sores; in insufficiency, it makes the heart vexed, with coughing and spitting appearing above, and an outflow of qi below. Huangdi said: Good.

The autumn pulse is like a floating pulse — in what way is it floating? Qibo said: The autumn pulse corresponds to the lung, to metal of the west, the direction by which the myriad things are gathered in and brought to completion. Therefore its qi comes light, empty, and floating, arriving quickly and dispersing as it leaves — hence it is called "floating." Anything contrary to this is disease. Huangdi said: What is meant by contrary to this? Qibo said: If the qi comes feather-like yet firm at the center and empty on both sides, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if the qi comes feather-like and faint, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: In excess and insufficiency of the autumn pulse, what are the diseases like in each case? Qibo said: In excess, it makes the qi rebel upward and the back ache, with a stifled, brooding feeling; in insufficiency, it makes a person pant, breathe with insufficient qi, and cough, with blood appearing when qi rises, and an abnormal sound heard below. Huangdi said: Good.

The winter pulse is like the "ying" [entrenched] pulse — in what way is it entrenched? Qibo said: The winter pulse corresponds to the kidney, to water of the north, the direction by which the myriad things are gathered together and stored away. Therefore its qi comes deep and beats hard — hence it is called "entrenched." Anything contrary to this is disease. Huangdi said: What is meant by contrary to this? Qibo said: If the qi comes like a stone flicked by the finger, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if it departs in a hurried, racing manner, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: In excess and insufficiency of the winter pulse, what are the diseases like in each case? Qibo said: In excess, it makes a person listless and weak, with pain along the spine and shortness of qi so that one does not wish to speak; in insufficiency, it makes the heart feel suspended as if famished, coldness in the region below the ribs, pain in the spine, fullness in the lower abdomen, and abnormal urination. Huangdi said: Good.

Huangdi said: The order of the four seasons and the changes of compliance and rebellion follow this pattern — but what alone governs the spleen pulse? Qibo said: The spleen pulse corresponds to earth, the solitary viscus that irrigates the four surrounding [viscera]. Huangdi said: Then can the good and the ill of the spleen be observed? Qibo said: The good cannot be seen; the ill can be seen. Huangdi said: In what way can the ill be seen? Qibo said: If it comes like flowing water, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if it is like the beak of a bird, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: Master, you say the spleen is the solitary viscus, earth at the center that irrigates the four surrounding [viscera] — what are the diseases of its excess and insufficiency like? Qibo said: In excess, it makes the four limbs unable to be raised; in insufficiency, it makes the nine orifices fail to be free, called "doubled stiffness." Huangdi rose in astonishment, bowed twice, touched his head to the ground, and said: Excellent. I have grasped the great essentials of the pulse, the utmost calculations of the world, the transformations of the five colors and the pulse, the estimation of the ordinary and the extraordinary. The Way lies in unity: the spirit turns without reversing; if it reverses, it no longer turns, and then the pivotal moment is lost. The essentials of these utmost calculations are close at hand yet subtle. Let them be inscribed on jade tablets and stored in the treasury of the viscera and bowels, to be read every morning — this is called "the Jade Mechanism."

Each viscus receives qi from the viscus that generates it, transmits it to the viscus it overcomes, and its qi lodges in the viscus that generates it, but death occurs at the viscus that cannot overcome it. When a disease is about to bring death, it must first be transmitted onward until it reaches the viscus that cannot overcome it, and only then does death occur. This describes the reverse movement of qi, and hence death results. The liver receives qi from the heart, transmits it to the spleen, and its qi lodges in the kidney; death occurs at the lung. The heart receives qi from the spleen, transmits it to the lung, and its qi lodges in the liver; death occurs at the kidney. The spleen receives qi from the lung, transmits it to the kidney, and its qi lodges in the heart; death occurs at the liver. The lung receives qi from the kidney, transmits it to the liver, and its qi lodges in the spleen; death occurs at the heart. The kidney receives qi from the liver, transmits it to the heart, and its qi lodges in the lung; death occurs at the spleen. These are all cases of death from reverse transmission. Dividing a day and night into five parts allows one to divine the early or late hour of death.

Huangdi said: The five viscera communicate with one another, and their transmission always follows a sequence. When one of the five viscera has disease, it transmits the disease to the one it overcomes. If left untreated, the rule is that in three months or six months, or in three days or six days, the disease will have been transmitted through the five viscera and death will occur — this is the sequence of transmission following the order of overcoming. Hence it is said: by distinguishing what pertains to yang, one knows whence the disease has come; by distinguishing what pertains to yin, one knows the time of death or survival. This means knowing that the patient dies upon reaching the viscus that constrains him.

Therefore wind is the chief of the hundred diseases. Now when wind and cold lodge in a person, they make the fine body hairs stand erect, close the skin, and produce heat; at this time, one may induce sweating to release it, or, if there is numbness, insensitivity, swelling and pain, one may use hot decoctions, fomentation, moxibustion, or needling to remove it. If left untreated, the disease enters and lodges in the lung, called "lung bi [impediment]," bringing on cough and rising qi. If left untreated, the lung then transmits it onward to the liver, and the disease is called "liver bi," also called "reversal," with rib-side pain and the bringing up of food; at this time, one may treat by pressing or by needling. If left untreated, the liver transmits it to the spleen, and the disease is called "spleen wind," bringing on jaundice, heat in the abdomen, a vexed heart, and yellow discharge; at this time, one may treat by pressing, by medicine, or by bathing. If left untreated, the spleen transmits it to the kidney, and the disease is called "mounting-conglomeration," with oppressive heat and pain in the lower abdomen and a whitish discharge, also called "gu [parasitic] disease"; at this time, one may treat by pressing or by medicine. If left untreated, the kidney transmits it to the heart, and the sinews and vessels pull against one another and grow tense — the disease is called "convulsion"; at this time, one may treat by moxibustion or by medicine. If left untreated, when ten full days have passed, by rule death should occur. Alternatively, the kidney may transmit it to the heart, and the heart in turn transmits it back to the lung, bringing on chills and fever, and by rule death should occur within three years — this is the sequence of the disease.

However, when a disease breaks out suddenly, it need not follow this order of transmission; or its transmission and transformation may fail to follow the sequence — when it does not enter in sequence, it is because worry, fear, sorrow, joy, and anger prevent it from following its proper order, and so a person develops serious illness. Thus, if joy causes great depletion, then kidney qi will take advantage of the weakness; anger causes liver qi to take advantage; sorrow causes lung qi to take advantage; fear causes spleen qi to take advantage; worry causes heart qi to take advantage — this is the principle involved. Hence there are five types of disease, and five times five makes twenty-five variations, along with their transmissions and transformations. "Transmission" here is another name for "taking advantage of weakness."

When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, breathing becomes labored, and this disturbance of qi is visible in the body's movement, death is expected within six months; when the true visceral pulse appears, then the day can be predicted. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, breathing becomes labored, and there is internal pain drawing into the shoulders and neck, death is expected within one month; when the true viscus pulse appears, then the day can be predicted. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, breathing becomes labored, and there is internal pain drawing into the shoulders and neck, death is expected within one month; when the true viscus pulse appears, then the day can be predicted. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, breathing becomes labored, there is internal pain drawing into the shoulders and neck, the body is hot, the flesh wastes and the muscle masses break down, when the true viscus pulse appears, death occurs within ten months. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, the marrow within the shoulder wastes away, movement grows ever more feeble, when the true viscus pulse comes and appears, death is expected within one year; once the true viscus pulse is seen, then the day can be predicted. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, there is pain within the abdomen, discomfort in the heart region, heat in the shoulders, neck and body, the muscle masses break down and the flesh wastes, the eye sockets sink in, when the true viscus pulse appears and the eyes no longer recognize people, death is immediate; if the eyes still recognize people, death occurs when the season arrives that the affected viscus cannot overcome. In cases of urgent depletion where collapse strikes the body suddenly, the five viscera become sealed and closed off, the pathways of the pulse are blocked, and qi no longer comes and goes — this is like falling and drowning, and no fixed time of death can be given. When the pulse is cut off and does not come, or when a person's breath produces five or six beats of the pulse in a single respiration, yet the flesh and muscle have not wasted away, then even though the true visceral pulse does not appear, death will still occur. When the true liver pulse arrives, it is tense both within and without, like running one's finger along the edge of a blade, hard and unyielding, like pressing on the strings of a zither or lute; the complexion is greenish-white and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. When the true heart pulse arrives, it is hard and pounding, like fingering the beads of Job's-tears seed strung one after another; the complexion is reddish-black and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. When the true lung pulse arrives, it is large and empty, like feathers brushing against a person's skin; the complexion is whitish-red and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. When the true kidney pulse arrives, it pounds and then breaks off, like a finger flicking a stone, hard and abrupt; the complexion is blackish-yellow and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. When the true spleen pulse arrives, it is weak, now rapid and now sparse; the complexion is yellowish-green and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. Whenever any of the true visceral pulses appear, death follows in every case, and there is no treating it. Huangdi said: Why is it said that the appearance of the true visceral pulse means death? Qibo said: All five viscera receive their qi from the stomach; the stomach is the root of the five viscera. The qi of the viscera cannot reach the Hand Taiyin channel by itself — it must rely on the qi of the stomach in order to reach the Hand Taiyin. Hence each of the five viscera, at its proper season, arrives of itself at the Hand Taiyin only by means of stomach qi. Therefore, when pathogenic qi prevails, the essential qi declines; and when a disease is severe, the stomach qi cannot accompany the visceral qi in reaching the Hand Taiyin together — so the qi of the true viscus appears alone. When it appears alone, the disease has overcome the viscus, and hence death is said to follow. Huangdi said: Good.

Huangdi said: Whenever treating disease, one must examine the body's physique and qi, the complexion and its luster, the strength or weakness of the pulse, and whether the disease is new or old, and then treat it without letting the proper moment pass. When physique and qi correspond to each other, this is called treatable; when the complexion has a floating luster, this is called easily cured; when the pulse follows the pattern of the four seasons, this is called treatable; when the pulse is weak yet smooth, this indicates the presence of stomach qi, called easily treated, and one should select the treatment according to the season. When physique and qi fail to correspond, this is called difficult to treat; when the complexion is withered and without luster, this is called difficult to cure; when the pulse is full and hard, this is called worsening; when the pulse runs contrary to the four seasons, this is called untreatable. One must examine these four difficulties and state them clearly. What is meant by "running contrary to the four seasons" is: in spring obtaining the lung pulse, in summer obtaining the kidney pulse, in autumn obtaining the heart pulse, in winter obtaining the spleen pulse — when these arrive suspended, cut off, sunken, and rough, this is called running contrary to the four seasons. Even when there is not yet a visible visceral form of disease, if in spring and summer the pulse is sunken and rough, or in autumn and winter the pulse is floating and large, this too is called running contrary to the four seasons. When there is heat disease yet the pulse is calm; when there is diarrhea yet the pulse is large; when blood has been lost yet the pulse is full; when the disease is internal yet the pulse is full and hard; when the disease is external yet the pulse is not full and hard — in all these cases, the disease is difficult to treat.

Huangdi said: I have heard that deficiency and excess are used to determine death and survival; I would like to hear the particulars. Qibo said: The five excesses bring death, and the five deficiencies bring death. Huangdi said: I would like to hear of the five excesses and five deficiencies. Qibo said: A full pulse, hot skin, a distended abdomen, blockage of both the front and back passages, and oppression with clouded vision — these are called the five excesses. A fine pulse, cold skin, insufficient qi, diarrhea and incontinence of both front and back passages, and inability to take in food and drink — these are called the five deficiencies. Huangdi said: Yet sometimes at such times a person survives — why is that? Qibo said: If thin gruel enters the stomach and the diarrhea stops, then even one with deficiency will live; if the body sweats and the bowels move freely afterward, then even one with excess will live. These are the signs that indicate survival.

素问.title_en: Treatise on the Three Sections and Nine Indicators
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have learned from you, Master, about the nine needles — so many and so vast that they cannot be exhausted. I wish to hear the essential Way, so that I may bequeath it to my sons and grandsons, transmit it to later generations, inscribe it in my very marrow, and store it within my liver and lungs; I will receive it with a blood oath and dare not disclose it carelessly. Let it accord with the Way of Heaven, having necessarily a beginning and an end, corresponding above to the brilliance of Heaven, the stars, and the calendrical order, and matching below the four seasons and the five phases, with noble and humble alternating in turn, winter belonging to yin and summer to yang — how does the human being correspond to this? I wish to hear the method. Qibo replied: How subtle a question this is! This concerns the utmost calculations of Heaven and Earth. Huangdi said: I wish to hear how the utmost calculations of Heaven and Earth correspond to the human form, so that blood and qi may be made to flow freely, and death and survival determined — how is this done? Qibo said: The utmost calculations of Heaven and Earth begin at one and end at nine. One is Heaven, two is Earth, three is Man; multiplying this by three, three times three makes nine, corresponding to the nine fields of Heaven. Therefore the human being has three sections, and each section has three indicators, by which death and survival are determined, the hundred diseases are managed, deficiency and excess are regulated, and pathogenic illness is removed.

Huangdi said: What are meant by the three sections? Qibo said: There is a lower section, a middle section, and an upper section; each section has three indicators. The three indicators are Heaven, Earth, and Man — one must point to them and guide the examination in order to obtain the true reading. In the upper section, Heaven is the pulsing vessel at the two temples; the Earth of the upper section is the pulsing vessel at the two temples; the Man of the upper section is the pulsing vessel in front of the ears. In the middle section, Heaven is the Hand Taiyin channel; the Earth of the middle section is the Hand Yangming channel; the Man of the middle section is the Hand Shaoyin channel. In the lower section, Heaven is the Foot Jueyin channel; the Earth of the lower section is the Foot Shaoyin channel; the Man of the lower section is the Foot Taiyin channel. Thus, the Heaven of the lower section indicates the liver, its Earth indicates the kidney, and its Man indicates the qi of the spleen and stomach. Huangdi said: What of the indicators of the middle section? Qibo said: There too are Heaven, Earth, and Man. Heaven indicates the lung, Earth indicates the qi within the chest, and Man indicates the heart. Huangdi said: By what are the indicators of the upper section determined? Qibo said: There too are Heaven, Earth, and Man. Heaven indicates the qi of the head and temples, Earth indicates the qi of the mouth and teeth, and Man indicates the qi of the ears and eyes. Thus each of the three sections has its Heaven, its Earth, and its Man. Three make a Heaven, three make an Earth, three make a Man; multiplying three by three gives nine in total; the nine are divided into the nine fields, and the nine fields correspond to the nine viscera. Hence there are five spirit-viscera and four form-viscera, together making the nine viscera. When the five viscera have already failed, the complexion will necessarily be withered, and when withered, death necessarily follows. Huangdi said: How does one determine this? Qibo said: One must first gauge whether the body's physique is fat or thin, in order to regulate the deficiency or excess of its qi — if there is excess, drain it; if there is deficiency, supplement it. One must first clear the blood vessels and only then regulate the qi, regardless of what the disease is, taking balance as the goal.

Huangdi said: How does one determine death and survival? Qibo said: When the physique is full but the pulse fine, and the breath insufficient to sustain respiration, this is dangerous. When the physique is thin but the pulse large, and there is excess qi within the chest, this means death. When physique and qi correspond to one another, this means life. When the pulses are irregular and uncoordinated, this means disease. When the three sections and nine indicators all fail to correspond with one another, this means death. When the pulses above and below, left and right, respond to each other like the alternating strokes of a pestle pounding grain, the disease is severe. When above and below, left and right, are so out of correspondence that they cannot be counted together, this means death. When the indicator of the middle section, though regulated by itself, fails to correspond with the other viscera, this means death. When the indicators of the middle section diminish relative to one another, this means death. When the eyes are sunken inward, this means death. Huangdi said: How does one know where the disease is located? Qibo said: When examining the nine indicators, if one alone is small, there is disease; if one alone is large, there is disease; if one alone is rapid, there is disease; if one alone is slow, there is disease; if one alone is hot, there is disease; if one alone is cold, there is disease; if one alone is sunken and depressed, there is disease. Press with the left hand on the leg, five inches above the ankle, and then flick near the ankle with the right hand — if the response travels upward beyond five inches in a wriggling motion, there is no disease; if the response is rapid, and under the hand feels turbid and rolling, there is disease; if under the hand it feels sluggish and slow, there is disease; if the response does not reach up to five inches, or flicking produces no response at all, this means death. Hence, when the flesh has wasted away and the body cannot move, this means death. When the middle section is now sparse and now rapid, this means death. When the pulse is intermittent and hooked, the disease lies in the network vessels. In the mutual correspondence of the nine indicators, above and below should be as one, and must not fail to correspond. When one indicator lags, there is disease; when two indicators lag, the disease is severe; when three indicators lag, the disease is critical. What is meant by "lagging" is that the responses are not simultaneous. Examine the bowels and viscera in order to know the timing of death and survival.

One must first understand the channels and vessels, and only then can one understand the pulse of disease. When the pulse of the true viscus appears, the disease overcomes the person and death results. When the qi of the Foot Taiyang channel is cut off, the feet cannot be flexed or extended, and at death the eyes will necessarily roll upward. Huangdi said: How is it that winter belongs to yin and summer to yang? Qibo said: Among the pulses of the nine indicators, those that are sunken, fine, suspended, and cut off belong to yin, governed by winter, and hence death occurs at midnight. Those that are full, agitated, panting, and rapid belong to yang, governed by summer, and hence death occurs at midday. Therefore, in cold-heat disease, death occurs at dawn. In internal heat and heat disease, death occurs at midday. In wind disease, death occurs at dusk. In water disease, death occurs at midnight. When the pulse is now sparse, now rapid, now slow, now hasty, death occurs at the turning of the four seasons. If the flesh has already wasted away, even though the nine indicators are regulated, death still occurs. If the seven signs of fatal prognosis appear but the nine indicators all comply with the norm, the patient does not die. What is meant by "not dying" refers to diseases of wind qi and diseases that run their course over a month, which resemble the diseases of the seven signs but are not truly so — hence it is said they do not die. But if the seven signs of disease are truly present and the pulse indicators are also in decay, then death follows, and hiccup and belching will necessarily occur. One must carefully inquire into how the disease began and what is presently afflicting the patient, and only then, in turn, palpate and trace each pulse, observing whether the channels and network vessels are floating or sunken, and follow them up and down, with the flow or against it. If the pulse is brisk, there is no disease; if the pulse is slow, there is disease; if the pulse neither comes nor goes, this means death; if the skin adheres, wasted and inelastic, this means death.

Huangdi said: How does one treat the cases that can be treated? Qibo said: When the disease is in the main channels, treat the main channels; when the disease is in the grandchild network vessels, treat the blood of the grandchild network vessels; when the disease is in the blood and the body has pain, treat the channels and network vessels. When the disease lies in an unusual pathogen, needle the unusual pathogen's vessel using contralateral needling. When the pathogen lingers and wastes the flesh without moving, needle at the joints. When there is excess above and deficiency below, palpate and follow it, seek out the knotted network vessels, needle them to let out the blood, and thereby open the passage so that it can be seen to flow freely. When the pupils are raised upward, this is insufficiency of the Taiyang; when the eyes roll fully upward and fixed, the Taiyang qi has already been cut off — this is essential to determining death and survival, and must not go unexamined. At the fingers, and five finger-widths above the outer ankle of the wrist, retain the needle.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Variances in the Channels and Vessels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Do a person's dwelling and posture, movement and rest, courage and timidity also cause the pulse to change accordingly? Qibo replied: In general, a person's fright, fear, anger, exertion, movement, and rest all cause change. Thus, when walking at night, panting arises from the kidney, and the overflowing qi brings disease to the lung. When there is a fall causing fear, panting arises from the liver, and the overflowing qi harms the spleen. When there is fright and fear, panting arises from the lung, and the overflowing qi injures the heart. When fording water and stumbling, panting arises from the kidney and the bones; at such times, in the courageous the qi moves on and the matter ends there, but in the timid it lodges and becomes disease. Hence it is said: the way of diagnosing disease is to observe a person's courage or timidity, and the state of the bones, flesh, and skin — being able to know these particulars constitutes the method of diagnosis. Thus, when eating and drinking to excessive fullness, sweat issues from the stomach. When startled so that essence is lost, sweat issues from the heart. When carrying heavy loads on a long journey, sweat issues from the kidney. When running swiftly in fear, sweat issues from the liver. When exerting the body in toil, sweat issues from the spleen. Therefore, throughout spring, autumn, winter, and summer, the yin and yang of the four seasons — disease arises from excessive use, and this is the constant rule.

When the qi of food enters the stomach, its essence is dispersed to the liver, and the overflowing qi extends to the sinews. The overflowing qi also enters the stomach; its turbid qi returns to the heart, and its overflowing essence extends to the vessels. The qi of the vessels flows through the channels, and the qi of the channels returns to the lung; the lung gathers all the vessels as at court and transports essence to the skin and body hair. The qi of the hair and vessels unite their essence and move qi to the bowels. The essence-qi of the bowels, luminous and clear, is retained in the four viscera, and this qi returns to the balance-beam of the pulse. When the balance is even, the qi opening at the wrist measures its proper inch, by which death and survival are determined. When drink enters the stomach, its overflowing essence-qi is transported upward to the spleen. The spleen qi disperses the essence, sending it upward to the lung, which regulates and clears the waterways, sending it downward to the bladder. The essence of water is thereby distributed to the four quarters, and the five channels move together in accord with it, in accordance with the four seasons and the yin and yang of the five viscera — estimating and measuring these is the constant standard.

When the Taiyang viscus-pulse arrives alone, there is reversal, panting, and deficient qi rebelling upward — this is insufficiency of yin and surplus of yang; both the exterior and interior should be drained together, taking points at the lower transport points. When the Yangming viscus-pulse arrives alone, this is a doubling and compounding of yang qi; one should drain the yang and supplement the yin, taking points at the lower transport points. When the Shaoyang viscus-pulse arrives alone, this is reversal qi, with the pulse at the yin heel vessel in front suddenly enlarged; take points at the lower transport points — when Shaoyang arrives alone, this is an excess of the one yang. When the Taiyin viscus-pulse beats abnormally, one must apply the mind carefully to discern the truth of the case: when the qi of the five pulses is scant and the stomach qi is not level, this is the three yin; one should treat the lower transport points, supplementing yang and draining yin. When the one yang howls alone, this is reversal of the Shaoyang; yang is doubled above, the four vessels contend and stretch taut, and the qi returns to the kidney; one should treat the channels and network vessels, draining yang and supplementing yin. When the one yin arrives, this is a matter of the Jueyin; there is true deficiency with a painful oppression at the heart, reversal qi lingering and pressing, manifesting as pale, cold sweat; regulate the diet and harmonize the medicine, with treatment at the lower transport points.
