Huangdi said: What is the image of the Taiyang viscus-pulse? Qibo said: It resembles the three yang, and is floating. Huangdi said: What is the image of the Shaoyang viscus-pulse? Qibo said: It resembles the one yang; the one yang viscus-pulse is smooth and slippery, yet not full. Huangdi said: What is the image of the Yangming viscus-pulse? Qibo said: It resembles being large and floating. The beating of the Taiyin viscus-pulse is described as hidden yet drumming. When the two yin beat and arrive, this is the kidney, sunken and not floating.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Qi of the Five Viscera Patterned on the Seasons
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: In according the human body with the pattern of the four seasons and the five phases in treatment, what course follows the natural order and what course goes against it? I wish to hear the principles of gain and loss in this matter. Qibo replied: The five phases are metal, wood, water, fire, and earth. They alternately wax and wane in ascendancy, by which one may know death and life, determine success and failure, and fix the timing of the ebb and flow of qi in the five viscera — the periods of remission and exacerbation, and the times of death and life. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the whole of it. Qibo said: The liver governs spring; the foot jueyin and shaoyang channels govern its treatment; its days are jia and yi (the first two of the ten Heavenly Stems). The liver suffers from being drawn up tight, so one should promptly eat sweet foods to relax it. The heart governs summer; the hand shaoyin and taiyang channels govern its treatment; its days are bing and ding. The heart suffers from laxity, so one should promptly eat sour foods to constrict it. The spleen governs long summer; the foot taiyin and yangming channels govern its treatment; its days are wu and ji. The spleen suffers from dampness, so one should promptly eat sweet foods to relax it. The heart governs summer; the hand shaoyin and taiyang channels govern its treatment; its days are bing and ding. The heart suffers from laxity, so one should promptly eat sour foods to constrict it. The spleen governs long summer; the foot taiyin and yangming channels govern its treatment; its days are wu and ji. The spleen suffers from dampness, so one should promptly eat bitter foods to dry it. The lung governs autumn; the hand taiyin and yangming channels govern its treatment; its days are geng and xin. The lung suffers from qi rebelling upward, so one should promptly eat bitter foods to drain it. The kidney governs winter; the foot shaoyin and taiyang channels govern its treatment; its days are ren and gui. The kidney suffers from dryness, so one should promptly eat pungent foods to moisten it, opening the interstices, bringing forth fluids, and freeing the flow of qi.

When disease is in the liver, it resolves in summer; if it does not resolve in summer, it worsens in autumn; if it does not cause death in autumn, it stabilizes in winter and flares up again in spring. The patient should avoid exposure to wind. In liver disease, recovery occurs on the days bing and ding; if not resolved on bing and ding, it worsens on geng and xin; if it does not cause death on geng and xin, it stabilizes on ren and gui, and flares up again on jia and yi. In liver disease, the patient is clearer at dawn, worsens in the late afternoon, and is calm at midnight. The liver desires to disperse; one should promptly eat pungent foods to disperse it — use pungent flavor to supplement it and sour flavor to drain it.

When disease is in the heart, it resolves in long summer; if it does not resolve in long summer, it worsens in winter; if it does not cause death in winter, it stabilizes in spring and flares up again in summer. The patient should avoid warm food and hot clothing. In heart disease, recovery occurs on wu and ji; if not resolved on wu and ji, it worsens on ren and gui; if it does not cause death on ren and gui, it stabilizes on jia and yi, and flares up again on bing and ding. In heart disease, the patient is clearer at midday, worsens at midnight, and is calm at dawn. The heart desires softness; one should promptly eat salty foods to soften it — use salty flavor to supplement it and sweet flavor to drain it.

When disease is in the spleen, it resolves in autumn; if it does not resolve in autumn, it worsens in spring; if it does not cause death in spring, it stabilizes in summer and flares up again in long summer. The patient should avoid warm food, overeating, damp ground, and wet clothing. In spleen disease, recovery occurs on geng and xin; if not resolved on geng and xin, it worsens on jia and yi; if it does not cause death on jia and yi, it stabilizes on bing and ding, and flares up again on wu and ji. In spleen disease, the patient is clearer in the early afternoon, worsens at sunrise, and is calm in the late afternoon. The spleen desires relaxation; one should promptly eat sweet foods to relax it — use bitter flavor to drain it and sweet flavor to supplement it.

When disease is in the lung, it resolves in winter; if it does not resolve in winter, it worsens in summer; if it does not cause death in summer, it stabilizes in long summer and flares up again in autumn. The patient should avoid cold food, cold drink, and cold clothing. In lung disease, recovery occurs on ren and gui; if not resolved on ren and gui, it worsens on bing and ding; if it does not cause death on bing and ding, it stabilizes on wu and ji, and flares up again on geng and xin. In lung disease, the patient is clearer in the late afternoon, worsens at midday, and is calm at midnight. The lung desires constriction; one should promptly eat sour foods to constrict it — use sour flavor to supplement it and pungent flavor to drain it.

When disease is in the kidney, it resolves in spring; if it does not resolve in spring, it worsens in long summer; if it does not cause death in long summer, it stabilizes in autumn and flares up again in winter. The patient should avoid searing heat, hot food, and warm, overheated clothing. In kidney disease, recovery occurs on jia and yi; if not resolved on jia and yi, it worsens on wu and ji; if it does not cause death on wu and ji, it stabilizes on geng and xin, and flares up again on ren and gui. In kidney disease, the patient is clearer at midnight, worsens at the turning points of the four seasons, and is calm in the late afternoon. The kidney desires firmness; one should promptly eat bitter foods to firm it — use bitter flavor to supplement it and salty flavor to drain it.

When pathogenic qi lodges in the body, it advances by way of the phase that overcomes the affected organ's phase; the disease resolves when it reaches the phase that the organ generates; it worsens when it reaches the phase that overcomes the organ; it stabilizes when it reaches the phase that generates the organ; and it flares up again when it reaches the organ's own proper phase-position. One must first determine the pulses of the five viscera before one can speak of the timing of remission and exacerbation, and the periods of death and life.

In liver disease, there is pain under both flanks pulling into the lower abdomen, causing the person to be prone to anger. In deficiency, the eyes are dim and see nothing, the ears hear nothing, and the person is prone to fear, as if someone were about to seize him. Select the jueyin and shaoyang channels. When the qi rebels upward, there is headache, deafness, impaired hearing, and swelling of the cheeks; bleed the channel.

In heart disease, there is pain in the chest, a bracing fullness in the flanks, pain under the flanks, pain in the chest, back, and between the shoulder blades, and pain along the inner arms. In deficiency, the chest and abdomen become distended, with pain pulling between the flanks and the lower back. Select the shaoyin and taiyang channels, and bleed under the tongue. For its complicated transformations, needle and bleed the Weizhong (Bend Center) point.

In spleen disease, the body feels heavy, the muscles tend toward wasting, the feet cannot be controlled, walking is often accompanied by spasms, and there is pain in the soles of the feet. In deficiency, the abdomen is distended and there is intestinal rumbling, with undigested food in loose stools. Select the taiyin, yangming, and shaoyin channels and bleed them.

In lung disease, there is panting and coughing with rebellious qi, pain in the shoulders and back, sweating, and pain throughout the buttocks, genitals, thighs, knees, hips, calves, shins, and feet. In deficiency, there is shortness of breath such that one cannot complete an exhalation, deafness, and dryness of the throat. Select the taiyin channel, the points outside the foot taiyin channel, and the points inside the jueyin channel, and bleed them.

In kidney disease, the abdomen is distended and the shins are swollen, with panting, coughing, and heaviness of the body; night sweats occur during sleep, and there is aversion to wind. In deficiency, there is pain in the chest, pain in the upper and lower abdomen, cold reversal of the limbs, and a despondent mood. Select the shaoyin and taiyang channels and bleed them.

The liver's color is blue-green; it is proper to eat sweet foods — polished rice, beef, jujubes, and mallow are all sweet. The heart's color is red; it is proper to eat sour foods — red beans, dog meat, plums, and chives are all sour. The lung's color is white; it is proper to eat bitter foods — wheat, mutton, apricots, and scallion-leek are all bitter. The spleen's color is yellow; it is proper to eat salty foods — soybeans, pork, chestnuts, and bean leaves are all salty. The kidney's color is black; it is proper to eat pungent foods — yellow millet, chicken, peaches, and scallions are all pungent. Pungent flavor disperses, sour flavor constricts, sweet flavor relaxes, bitter flavor firms, and salty flavor softens. Potent drugs attack pathogenic qi; the five grains provide nourishment; the five fruits give assistance; the five kinds of meat provide benefit; the five vegetables supply fullness. When qi and flavor are properly combined and taken, they supplement essence and boost qi. Of these five flavors — pungent, sour, sweet, bitter, and salty — each has its own benefit: some disperse, some constrict; some relax, some tighten; some firm, some soften. According to the four seasons and the five viscera, disease is treated according to whichever of the five flavors is appropriate.

素问.title_en: Elucidating the Five Qi
paragraphs_en: Where the flavors enter: sour enters the liver, pungent enters the lung, bitter enters the heart, salty enters the kidney, and sweet enters the spleen. This is called the five entries.

Where qi disorder causes disease: in the heart it causes belching, in the lung it causes coughing, in the liver it causes excessive talking, in the spleen it causes swallowing reflux, in the kidney it causes yawning and sneezing. In the stomach it causes rebellious qi, retching, and fear; in the large and small intestines it causes diarrhea; overflow in the lower burner causes water swelling; inhibited function of the bladder causes dribbling urinary blockage, and loss of control causes incontinence; in the gallbladder it causes anger. This is called the five diseases of qi.

Where essence-qi concentrates: when essence-qi concentrates in the heart, there is joy; when it concentrates in the lung, there is sorrow; when it concentrates in the liver, there is worry; when it concentrates in the spleen, there is apprehension; when it concentrates in the kidney, there is fear. This is called the five concentrations — they occur when an organ is deficient and qi concentrates within it.
What the viscera dislike: the heart dislikes heat, the lung dislikes cold, the liver dislikes wind, the spleen dislikes dampness, and the kidney dislikes dryness. This is called the five aversions.

What the viscera transform into fluids: the heart produces sweat, the lung produces nasal mucus, the liver produces tears, the spleen produces thin saliva, and the kidney produces thick spittle. This is called the five fluids.

Flavors to be avoided: pungent flavor moves to the qi, so in qi disease one should not eat much pungent food; salty flavor moves to the blood, so in blood disease one should not eat much salty food; bitter flavor moves to the bones, so in bone disease one should not eat much bitter food; sweet flavor moves to the flesh, so in flesh disease one should not eat much sweet food; sour flavor moves to the sinews, so in sinew disease one should not eat much sour food. This is called the five prohibitions — one must not overeat these.

Where diseases arise: yin disease arises in the bones, yang disease arises in the blood, yin disease arises in the flesh, yang disease arises in winter, yin disease arises in summer. This is called the five arisings.

Where pathogenic qi causes disorder: when pathogenic qi enters the yang, it causes mania; when it enters the yin, it causes impediment (bi-syndrome); when it clashes with the yang, it causes disease of the vertex; when it clashes with the yin, it causes muteness; when yang pathogenic qi enters the yin, the person becomes quiet; when yin pathogenic qi emerges into the yang, the person becomes angry. This is called the five disorders.

Where pathogenic pulses appear: in spring, finding the autumn pulse; in summer, finding the winter pulse; in long summer, finding the spring pulse; in autumn, finding the summer pulse; in winter, finding the long-summer pulse — these are called yin emerging into yang. The disease manifesting this way tends toward anger and is untreatable. This is called the five pathogenic pulses; all share the same fate — death, and cannot be treated.

What the viscera store: the heart stores the spirit, the lung stores the corporeal soul (po), the liver stores the ethereal soul (hun), the spleen stores intention, and the kidney stores will. This is called what the five viscera store.

What the viscera govern: the heart governs the vessels, the lung governs the skin, the liver governs the sinews, the spleen governs the flesh, and the kidney governs the bones. This is called the five governings.

What overexertion injures: prolonged looking injures the blood, prolonged lying injures the qi, prolonged sitting injures the flesh, prolonged standing injures the bones, prolonged walking injures the sinews. This is called the injuries of the five kinds of overexertion.

The pulse images corresponding to the viscera: the liver's pulse is string-like, the heart's pulse is hook-like, the spleen's pulse is intermittent, the lung's pulse is feather-like, and the kidney's pulse is stone-like. This is called the pulses of the five viscera.

素问.title_en: Blood and Qi, Body and Mind
paragraphs_en: As for the constant proportions in man: the taiyang channels normally have abundant blood and little qi; the shaoyang channels normally have little blood and abundant qi; the yangming channels normally have abundant qi and abundant blood; the shaoyin channels normally have little blood and abundant qi; the jueyin channels normally have abundant blood and little qi; the taiyin channels normally have abundant qi and little blood. These are the constant proportions ordained by heaven. The foot taiyang and shaoyin are exterior-interior pairs, the shaoyang and jueyin are exterior-interior pairs, and the yangming and taiyin are exterior-interior pairs — these are the yin and yang of the foot channels. The hand taiyang and shaoyin are exterior-interior pairs, the shaoyang and the pericardium (heart-ruler) are exterior-interior pairs, and the yangming and taiyin are exterior-interior pairs — these are the yin and yang of the hand channels. Now, knowing what afflicts the hand and foot yin and yang channels, in treating disease one must first remove excess blood, and then remove what afflicts the patient, attending to what the qi desires, and only then drain what is in excess and supplement what is deficient.

To find the back transport (shu) points: first measure the distance between the two nipples, and fold the measuring cord in half at the middle point. Then take another stalk and measure off half of that folded length, so that the two ends of this shorter piece, when joined at right angles with the first, form a set-square. Then raise it to measure along the back, positioning one corner at the top, level with the Da Zhui point on the spine, with the other two corners below. The point at the lower corner is the lung's transport point. Measuring one further increment down is the heart's transport point. Measuring one further increment down, the left corner is the liver's transport point and the right corner is the spleen's transport point. Measuring one further increment down is the kidney's transport point. These are called the transport points of the five viscera, the measurements for moxibustion and needling.

When the body is at ease but the mind is troubled, disease arises in the vessels; treat it with moxibustion and needling. When the body is at ease and the mind is at ease, disease arises in the flesh; treat it with needles and stone probes. When the body is troubled but the mind is at ease, disease arises in the sinews; treat it with hot compresses and guided stretching exercises. When the body is troubled and the mind is troubled, disease arises in the throat; treat it with the hundred medicines. When the body is repeatedly startled and frightened, the channels and network vessels become blocked, and disease arises as numbness; treat it with massage and medicated wine. These are called the five combinations of body and mind.

In needling the yangming channels, bleed and let out qi. In needling the taiyang channels, bleed but avoid letting out qi. In needling the shaoyang channels, let out qi but avoid bleeding. In needling the taiyin channels, let out qi but avoid bleeding. In needling the shaoyin channels, let out qi but avoid bleeding. In needling the jueyin channels, bleed but avoid letting out qi.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Treasuring Life and Preserving the Bodily Form
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Heaven covers and earth carries all things; the ten thousand things are all complete within this cosmos, yet nothing is more precious than man. Man is born from the qi of heaven and earth, and comes to maturity according to the pattern of the four seasons. Rulers and commoners alike all wish to preserve their bodily form intact, yet when disease afflicts the body, none understand its true nature; it lingers and spreads, growing deeper day by day, until it lodges in the bone marrow. This troubles my heart. I wish to use needling to remove such disease — how should this be done? Qibo replied: When salt has a salty flavor, its qi will cause the vessel containing it to seep moisture; when a bowstring is about to snap, its sound becomes hoarse and broken; when a tree is diseased, its leaves wither; when a person's disease is deep, his voice becomes broken and retching. When a person shows these three signs, this is called a ruined organ-system — potent drugs cannot treat it, and short needles cannot draw it out. In all these cases, the skin is severed and the flesh damaged, and blood and qi struggle and turn dark.

Huangdi said: I am troubled thinking of their suffering; my heart is thrown into confusion and distress over it, for the disease is only worsened and cannot be undone. When the common people hear of this, they take the physician for a maiming brigand. What is to be done? Qibo said: Man is born of the earth and his life is suspended from heaven; when the qi of heaven and earth combine, this is called man. One who can respond to the four seasons has heaven and earth as father and mother; one who understands the ten thousand things is called the Son of Heaven. Heaven has yin and yang; man has the twelve joints. Heaven has cold and heat; man has deficiency and excess. One who can trace through the transformations of yin and yang in heaven and earth will not lose step with the four seasons; one who understands the principles of the twelve joints cannot be deceived even by sage wisdom. One who can retain understanding of the changes of the eight movements of qi, by which the five phases' ascendancies alternately establish themselves, and who can penetrate the numerical patterns of deficiency and excess, moves alone and acts alone — even the faintest sigh or moan is perceived, and the finest hair of autumn is visible to his eye.

Huangdi said: Man's life has bodily form and cannot be separated from yin and yang. Heaven and earth combine their qi and differentiate into the nine regions, and divide into the four seasons; months have their short and long durations, days have their short and long lengths; the ten thousand things arrive together in such abundance that they cannot be fully measured, manifesting as deficiency and excess, sighing and moaning. May I ask about the method of treating this? Qibo said: Wood, meeting metal, is felled; fire, meeting water, is extinguished; earth, meeting wood, is penetrated; metal, meeting fire, is notched; water, meeting earth, is cut off. All the ten thousand things are thus, and this cannot be exhausted. Therefore there are five things about needling that hang displayed before all under heaven, as common as the leftover food shared among the common people, yet none understand them. The first is called governing the spirit; the second is knowing how to nourish the body; the third is knowing the true efficacy of medicinal substances; the fourth is knowing how to fashion stone probes of the proper size; the fifth is knowing how to diagnose the blood and qi of the bowels and viscera. When these five methods are all established, each has its priority. Now, in the needling practiced in this degenerate age, physicians merely supplement what is deficient and drain what is full — this is all that the multitude of practitioners commonly know. But as for modeling oneself on heaven and taking earth as one's law, moving in accord with what responds — true skill harmonizes as an echo answers a sound, and follows as a shadow follows a form; the Way has no ghosts or spirits in it, and the skilled physician comes and goes alone by his own understanding.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear about the Way of this. Qibo said: In all true needling, one must first govern the spirit. Once the state of the five viscera is determined and the nine indicators are complete, only then does one focus the mind on the needle — not distracted by the multitude of other pulses, not disturbed by hearing of the multitude of ill omens, exterior and interior brought into accord, without letting outward appearance take precedence. One may then observe the coming and going of qi, and only then apply the needle to the person. In man there is deficiency and excess: in the five deficiencies, do not approach carelessly; in the five excesses, do not hold back. When the moment for insertion arrives, there is no room even for the width of a hair between readiness and action. The hand moves as if intent on urgent work, the needle gleaming and even in its motion; with a calm mind, observe the meaning of the pulse, and watch for the changes indicating the proper moment. This is called the imperceptible and obscure — none can know its form. One sees it as crows gathering in a flock, one sees it swarming densely, one then sees it take flight, yet knows not what it is; at rest it is like a crossbow drawn and held level, in motion like the release of its trigger. Huangdi said: What is meant by deficiency, and what is meant by excess? Qibo said: In needling deficiency, one waits for the qi to become replete; in needling excess, one waits for it to become drained. Once the channel qi has arrived, guard it carefully and do not lose it. The choice of depth and shallowness lies in the intent of the physician; whether the site is far or near, the focus should be as one, undivided. Approach it as if overlooking a deep abyss; hold the hand as if grasping a tiger; let the spirit not be distracted by the multitude of other things.

素问.title_en: Discourse on the Eight Correct Periods and Spirit Illumination
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: In the practice of using needles, there must be rules and standards. What are these rules and standards? Qibo replied: One models on heaven and takes law from earth, according with the light of heaven — the sun, moon, and stars. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the whole of it. Qibo said: In the method of needling, one must always observe the qi of the sun, moon, stars, the four seasons, and the eight correct periods; only when the qi is settled should one needle. Therefore, when the weather is warm and the sun bright, people's blood becomes fluid and moist, and the defensive qi floats near the surface, so blood is easily drained and qi easily moves; when the weather is cold and the sky overcast, people's blood congeals and stagnates, and the defensive qi sinks. When the moon begins to wax, blood and qi begin to grow refined and the defensive qi begins to circulate; when the moon's disk is full, blood and qi become replete and the flesh becomes firm; when the moon's disk is empty, the flesh diminishes, the channels and network vessels become empty, the defensive qi departs, and the bodily form stands alone and unsupported. Therefore one adjusts blood and qi in accordance with the timing of heaven. For this reason, do not needle when the weather is cold, and do not hesitate to needle when the weather is warm. Do not drain when the moon is waxing; do not supplement when the moon is full; do not treat when the moon's disk is empty — this is called obtaining the proper time and regulating accordingly. Following the sequence of heaven and the times of abundance and depletion, one tracks the movement of light to fix one's position, and stands correctly to await the right moment. Therefore, to drain when the sun and moon are newly waxing is called emptying the viscera; to supplement when the moon is full causes blood and qi to overflow in excess, with stagnant blood lodging in the network vessels — this is called doubled excess; to treat when the moon's disk is empty is called disordering the channels. In such cases yin and yang become interlaced and confused, true qi and pathogenic qi cannot be distinguished, pathogenic qi sinks in and lingers, the exterior becomes deficient and the interior disordered, and excessive pathogenic influences then arise.

Huangdi said: What do the stars and the eight correct periods indicate? Qibo said: The stars are what regulate the movements of the sun and moon. The eight correct periods are what indicate the depleting pathogenic influence of the eight winds, which arrive at their proper times. The four seasons are what divide where the qi of spring, autumn, winter, and summer resides, so that one may regulate accordingly with the season; one must recognize the depleting pathogenic winds of the eight correct periods and avoid exposure to them. When bodily deficiency meets with heaven's deficiency, the two deficiencies resonate with each other, and this pathogenic qi penetrates to the bone; entering, it damages the five viscera. But if the practitioner watches for it and treats it in time, it cannot cause harm. Therefore it is said: the prohibitions of heaven must not go unknown.

Huangdi said: Good. I have now heard about modeling on the stars; I wish to hear about modeling on the ancients. Qibo said: To model on the ancients is first to know the classics of needling. To verify one's practice in the present day is first to know the cold and warmth of the days and the waxing and waning of the moon, so as to observe whether qi floats or sinks, and regulate it in the body, observing that this immediately proves effective. As for observing what is obscure and unseen — this refers to the fact that the bodily form, qi, and nutritive and defensive qi do not show themselves outwardly, yet the skilled practitioner alone knows them: by the cold and warmth of the day, the waning and waxing of the moon, and the floating and sinking of qi through the four seasons, examined together and cross-checked, he regulates accordingly. The skilled practitioner always perceives this beforehand, though it does not show outwardly — hence it is called observing what is obscure and unseen. One who penetrates the inexhaustible may transmit this knowledge to later generations; this is precisely wherein the skilled practitioner differs from others, yet since it does not show outwardly, others altogether cannot perceive it. Looked at, it has no form; tasted, it has no flavor — hence it is called the obscure and unseen, as if divine, as if numinous. As for depleting pathogenic qi — this is the depleting pathogenic qi of the eight correct periods. As for upright, ordinary pathogenic influence — when the body exerts effort, sweat is emitted, and the interstices open, and one then meets an ordinary depleting wind, it strikes the person only lightly, so that none can know its true nature nor see its form. The superior practitioner treats disease at its budding stage — he must first perceive the qi of the three sections and nine indicators, regulating fully before it has deteriorated, and thereby rescues the patient; hence he is called a superior practitioner. The inferior practitioner treats disease only after it has already formed, or after it has already broken down. Treating it after it has already formed means he does not know how the three sections and nine indicators have lost their proper coordination, and so the disease is allowed to progress to breakdown. Knowing where the disease lies means knowing how to diagnose the diseased pulse locations of the three sections and nine indicators and treat accordingly. Hence it is said: guard the gateways of the body — for none can know the pathogen's true nature, yet the skilled practitioner can perceive the pathogen's form.

Huangdi said: I have heard of supplementing and draining, but have not grasped their meaning. Qibo said: Draining must employ the 'square' method — 'square' meaning to act when the qi is just at its height, when the moon is just full, when the day is just warm, when the body is just settled, and when the breath is just inhaling — one inserts the needle then; then again one waits for the next inhalation to rotate the needle; then again one waits for the exhalation to slowly withdraw the needle. Hence it is said: draining must employ the 'square' timing, so that the pathogenic qi is made to move out. Supplementing must employ the 'round' method — 'round' meaning to move, and to move means to shift; the needling must strike the nutritive qi precisely, and the needle is then withdrawn upon inhalation. Thus 'round' and 'square' do not refer to the shape of the needle itself, but to the manner and timing of its use. Therefore, one who nourishes the spirit must know whether the body is fat or thin, and the abundance or decline of the nutritive and defensive qi and of blood and qi. Blood and qi are the person's spirit, and must not fail to be carefully nourished.

Huangdi said: How subtle and marvelous is this discourse! Uniting the human bodily form with yin and yang and the four seasons, the correspondences of deficiency and excess, and the timing of what is obscure and unseen — who but you, Master, could penetrate these things? Yet you have repeatedly spoken of form and spirit — what is meant by form, and what is meant by spirit? I wish to hear the whole of it. Qibo said: Let me speak of form. Form, ah, form — even when the eyes are dim and cannot see it directly, one asks about the patient's disease and seeks it in the channels: it becomes clearly present before one, yet when pressed for, it cannot be grasped, and its true nature remains unknown — hence it is called form. Huangdi said: What is meant by spirit? Qibo said: Let me speak of spirit. Spirit, ah, spirit — the ears do not hear it, but the eyes become clear and the heart opens, and the intention goes before; one comes to a sudden and solitary realization, which the mouth cannot put into words. All look upon it, yet the skilled practitioner alone perceives it; it is as if in dimness, and then suddenly and uniquely bright, like wind blowing away the clouds — hence it is called spirit. The three sections and nine indicators are its foundation; as for the discourses on the nine needles, they need not necessarily be retained.

素问.title_en: The Divergence and Convergence of True and Pathogenic Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard of the Nine Needles in nine chapters, and you, Master, multiplied this ninefold, into nine times nine — eighty-one chapters — and I have now thoroughly grasped their meaning. The classics speak of the waxing and waning of qi, its shifting to the left and right, of regulating below by means of above, regulating the right by means of the left, and of supplementing and draining the nutritive qi at the transport points according to excess and insufficiency — these I understand. But all of this concerns the shifting of nutritive and defensive qi, and how deficiency and excess arise from within — not pathogenic qi entering the channels from outside. I wish to hear about pathogenic qi being present in the channels: how does it afflict a person, and how is it treated? Qibo replied: When the sages established their measures and numbers, these necessarily corresponded to heaven and earth. Thus heaven has its lodge-degrees of the stars, earth has its rivers, and man has his channels and vessels. When heaven and earth are warm and mild, the rivers are calm and still; when heaven is cold and earth is frozen, the rivers congeal and stagnate; when heaven is hot and earth is scorching, the rivers boil and overflow; when a sudden violent wind arises, the rivers surge in waves and swell into mounds. So too, when pathogenic qi enters the vessels: in cold, the blood congeals and stagnates; in heat, the qi becomes fluid and moist; depleting pathogenic qi thereby enters and lodges in the channels, just as the rivers are affected by wind. The pulsing vessels of the channels, when pathogenic qi arrives, likewise at times swell into mounds; normally, qi travels through the vessels smoothly and continuously, but when it reaches the cunkou pulse under the examining hand, it is at times large and at times small — when large, pathogenic qi has arrived; when small, the pulse is calm. Its movement has no constant location; whether in yin or yang channels, it cannot be measured by fixed rule. One must trace and examine it through the three sections and nine indicators, and when one suddenly encounters it, one must block its path early. Insert the needle upon inhalation, so as not to let it clash with the flow of qi; hold it still and retain it for a long while, so as not to let the pathogenic qi spread; upon the next inhalation, rotate the needle, aiming to obtain the arrival of qi; wait for the exhalation and then withdraw the needle, removing it only once the exhalation is complete, so that the great pathogenic qi is entirely expelled — this is called draining. Huangdi said: How does one supplement what is insufficient? Qibo said: One must first press and trace along the vessel with the palm, pinch and disperse any obstruction, push and press upon it, flick it to make it swell up, scratch the skin and press the needle down, and open a path to insert it. Draw open its gateway from outside so as to close in its spirit within; insert the needle only upon completion of the exhalation, hold it still and retain it for a long while, aiming to obtain the arrival of qi — waiting as one would await something precious, unmindful of the lateness of the day, until the qi arrives. Then attend to it and guard it carefully; wait for the inhalation and then withdraw the needle, so that the qi cannot escape but remains each in its place; press the gateway closed, so that the spirit-qi is preserved and the great qi is retained and held in place — this is called supplementing.

Huangdi said: How does one watch for the arrival of qi? Qibo said: When pathogenic qi leaves the network vessels and enters the channels, it lodges within the blood vessels; before its coldness or warmth has settled with the body, it is like a surging wave rising up — coming at times, going at times, and thus not constantly present. Hence it is said: just as it arrives, one must press and stop it, stop it and then draw it out, but must not meet it head-on and drain it prematurely. True qi is the channel qi; if the channel qi is already quite deficient, one must not attack it as pathogenic qi arrives — this is the meaning of 'one must not meet it upon its arrival.' Hence it is said: if one watches for pathogenic qi without due care, and the great pathogenic qi has already passed, then to drain at that point would cause the true qi to be lost; once lost, it cannot be restored, and the pathogenic qi will return again, so that the disease accumulates further — hence it is said: 'one cannot pursue it after it has departed' — this is the meaning of that. As for the timing being so precise that it cannot be off even by the breadth of a hair: one must wait for the moment the pathogenic qi arrives and then release the needle to drain it; if one acts too early or too late, the blood and qi will already be exhausted, and the disease cannot then be brought down. Hence it is said: knowing when it can be drawn out is like releasing a trigger; not knowing how to draw it out is like knocking on a wooden post that will not sound. Hence it is said: one who knows the way of the critical moment will not be off even by the breadth of a hair; one who does not know the critical moment will knock and get no response — this is the meaning of that. Huangdi said: How is supplementing and draining done in this case? Qibo said: This is a matter of attacking pathogenic qi — one must swiftly let it out so as to remove the pooled blood and restore the true qi. When the pathogenic qi is newly lodged, it drifts about without a fixed location: pushed, it moves forward; pulled, it stops. Needle against its direction of movement — this warms the blood. Needle to let out its blood, and the disease will be resolved at once.

Huangdi said: Good. But when true qi and pathogenic qi have already merged, and no wave or mound rises — how does one examine for it then? Qibo said: Carefully press and trace along the three sections and nine indicators for abundance and deficiency, and regulate accordingly; observe where left and right, above and below, fail to correspond or are diminished relative to each other; carefully determine the diseased viscus and gauge the critical time accordingly. One who does not understand the three sections cannot distinguish yin from yang, nor differentiate heaven from earth. Among the nine indicators, the earth positions examine the earth, the heaven positions examine the heaven, and the man positions examine the man; one regulates them at the central organs, so as to fix the three sections. Hence it is said: if one needles without knowing the locations of the diseased pulses of the three sections and nine indicators, then even though a great excess arrives, the practitioner cannot prevent it. To punish where there is no fault is called great confusion — on the contrary it throws the great channels into disorder, so that the true qi cannot be restored; such a practitioner treats excess as deficiency and takes pathogenic qi for true qi; his use of the needle is without proper principle, and instead becomes a brigand against qi, robbing the person of their upright qi; he treats compliance as rebellion, so that nutritive and defensive qi scatter into disorder and true qi is already lost, while pathogenic qi alone remains lodged within — this cuts short the person's long life and brings upon him calamity from heaven. Not knowing the three sections and nine indicators, such a practitioner cannot help patients endure long. Because he does not know how to correlate treatment with the four seasons and the five phases, and misapplies the mutual-overcoming relations among the phases, he releases the pathogenic and attacks the upright, thereby cutting short the person's long life. When pathogenic qi is newly arrived as a guest in the body, it has as yet no fixed location: pushed, it moves forward; pulled, it stops. Meet it directly and drain it, and the disease will be resolved at once.

素问.title_en: Comprehensive Discourse on Deficiency and Excess
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What is meant by deficiency and excess? Qibo replied: When the pathogenic qi is exuberant, this is excess; when the essential qi is despoiled, this is deficiency. Huangdi said: What are deficiency and excess like? Qibo said: When qi is deficient, it is the lung that is deficient; when qi rebels upward, the feet become cold. If this occurs out of its proper season, the patient lives; if it occurs in its proper season, the patient dies. The same holds for the other viscera. Huangdi said: What is meant by double excess? Qibo said: What is called double excess refers to a case of great heat disease, where the qi is hot and the pulse is full — this is called double excess. Huangdi said: What of a case where both the channels and the network vessels are excess — how is it treated? Qibo said: When both channels and network vessels are excess, the inch pulse is tense while the cubit pulse is slack; both must be treated. Hence it is said: a slippery pulse indicates a favorable course, a rough pulse an adverse one. Deficiency and excess both follow from the nature of the affected substance; thus when the five viscera, bones, and flesh are smooth and well-ordered, one may enjoy long life. Huangdi said: What of a case where the qi of the network vessels is insufficient while the qi of the channels is surplus? Qibo said: When the network qi is insufficient and the channel qi is surplus, the pulse at the wrist opening is hot while the cubit is cold; this is adverse in autumn and winter, favorable in spring and summer — treat according to the governing disease. Huangdi said: What of a case where the channels are deficient and the network vessels full? Qibo said: When the channels are deficient and the network vessels full, the cubit is hot and full while the wrist-opening pulse is cold and rough; such a patient dies in spring and summer and lives in autumn and winter. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: When the network vessels are full and the channels deficient, moxa the yin and needle the yang; when the channels are full and the network vessels deficient, needle the yin and moxa the yang. Huangdi said: What is meant by double deficiency? Qibo said: When the pulse qi is deficient above and the cubit is deficient, this is called double deficiency. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: What is called deficient qi means the pulse lacks constancy. A deficient cubit means the patient walks with a faltering, unsteady gait. A deficient pulse means it does not resemble what is proper to yin. In such cases, a slippery pulse means life; a rough pulse means death.

Huangdi said: What of a case where cold qi suddenly surges upward and the pulse is full and excess? Qibo said: If excess is accompanied by a slippery pulse, the patient lives; if excess is accompanied by a rebellious pulse, the patient dies. Huangdi said: What of a case where the pulse is excess and full, the hands and feet are cold, and the head is hot? Qibo said: In spring and autumn the patient lives; in winter and summer the patient dies. If the pulse is floating and rough, and this roughness is accompanied by bodily heat, the patient dies. Huangdi said: What of a case where the whole body is swollen and full? Qibo said: When the whole body is full, the pulse is tense, large, and hard, while the cubit is rough and fails to respond; in such a case, a favorable course means life, an adverse course means death. Huangdi said: What is meant by a favorable course meaning life, and an adverse course meaning death? Qibo said: What is called favorable is when the hands and feet are warm. What is called adverse is when the hands and feet are cold. Huangdi said: What of a nursing infant afflicted with heat disease, whose pulse is suspended and small? Qibo said: If the hands and feet are warm, the child lives; if cold, the child dies. Huangdi said: What of a nursing infant struck by wind-heat, panting, wheezing, and heaving the shoulders in breath — what is the pulse like? Qibo said: When panting, wheezing, and heaving the shoulders, the pulse is excess and large; if moderate, the child lives; if tense, the child dies. Huangdi said: What of intestinal flux with blood in the stool? Qibo said: If the body is hot, the patient dies; if cold, the patient lives. Huangdi said: What of intestinal flux discharging white froth? Qibo said: If the pulse is sunken, the patient lives; if floating, the patient dies. Huangdi said: What of intestinal flux discharging pus and blood? Qibo said: If the pulse is suspended and cut off, the patient dies; if slippery and large, the patient lives. Huangdi said: What of cases akin to intestinal flux where the body is not hot and the pulse is not suspended and cut off? Qibo said: If the pulse is slippery and large, this indicates life; if suspended and rough, this indicates death — the time of crisis being determined according to the viscus involved. Huangdi said: What of epilepsy? Qibo said: If the pulse strikes large and slippery, the disease resolves of itself in time; if the pulse is small, hard, and tense, it is fatal and cannot be treated. Huangdi said: In the pulse of epilepsy, what of deficiency and excess? Qibo said: If deficient, it can be treated; if excess, the patient dies. Huangdi said: In wasting-thirst disease, what of deficiency and excess? Qibo said: If the pulse is excess and large, even a disease of long standing can be treated; if the pulse is suspended, small, and hard, a disease of long standing cannot be treated.

Huangdi said: As for the measurement of the body's form, the measurement of the bones, the measurement of the vessels, and the measurement of the sinews — how is one to know these measures? Huangdi said: In spring, one urgently treats the channels and network vessels; in summer, one urgently treats the channel transport points; in autumn, one urgently treats the six bowels; in winter, the pores are sealed shut. When they are sealed shut, one employs medicinal decoctions and uses needle and stone but sparingly. What is meant by using needle and stone sparingly does not apply to abscesses and welling-sores, for abscesses and welling-sores permit no delay of even a moment. When an abscess's location is unclear, so that pressing upon it evokes no response beneath the hand, and it comes and goes suddenly, needle three points beside the Hand Greater Yin channel, and two points each at the tasseled vessels. For a great, hot armpit abscess, needle five points of the Foot Lesser Yang; if after needling the heat does not subside, needle three points of the Hand Ruler of the Heart, and needle three points each at the meeting of the great bone along the Hand Greater Yin channel and its network vessels. For a sudden abscess with the sinews drawn taut, causing pain that follows the divisions of the flesh, with the essence-sweat not fully spent and the qi of the bladder insufficient, treatment lies at the channel transport points. For sudden abdominal fullness that does not yield to pressure, select the network vessel of the Hand Greater Yang, which is the alarm point of the stomach, and the transport point of the Lesser Yin, five fen to the side of the spine, three cun from the vertebrae, using the round, sharp needle. For sudden turmoil disorder, needle five points beside the transport points, and three points at the Foot Yang Brightness and above it to the side. For epilepsy and fright, needle five points of the vessel, needle five points each of the Hand Greater Yin, needle five points of the Greater Yang channel, needle one point beside the network vessel of the Hand Lesser Yin, one point of the Foot Yang Brightness, and needle three needles five cun above the ankle.

In general, when treating wasting-thirst, sudden collapse, hemiplegia, wilting and reversal, and fullness of qi with rebellious ascent — these are diseases of the rich and noble who feast on fine grain and rich meat. Obstruction and blockage in the region of the diaphragm, with upper and lower failing to communicate, are diseases born of sudden excessive worry. Sudden fainting together with deafness, and one-sided blockage and closure without free passage, arise from a sudden clash of the internal qi. Illnesses that arise neither from within nor from wind striking from without are those in which emaciation lingers and settles. Limping and lameness of the foot are diseases of cold, wind, and dampness. Huangdi said: Jaundice, sudden pain, epilepsy, fainting, and mania are generated by prolonged rebellious qi. Disharmony of the five viscera is generated by blockage and closure of the six bowels. Headache and ringing in the ears, with the nine orifices failing to function smoothly, are generated by disorders of the intestines and stomach.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Greater Yin and Yang Brightness
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Greater Yin and Yang Brightness stand in exterior-interior relation, being the vessels of the spleen and stomach; why then do the diseases they generate differ? Qibo replied: Yin and yang occupy different positions; they alternate between deficiency and excess, between rebellion and compliance; some diseases follow from within, others from without — since what they follow differs, the diseases bear different names. Huangdi said: I wish to hear of these differing manifestations. Qibo said: Yang is the qi of Heaven and governs the exterior; yin is the qi of Earth and governs the interior. Hence the way of yang is excess, the way of yin is deficient. Thus when one is struck by marauding wind and depleting evil, yang receives it; when eating and drinking are without moderation and rising and resting are without regularity, yin receives it. When yang receives it, the evil enters the six bowels; when yin receives it, the evil enters the five viscera. Entering the six bowels, the body becomes hot, one cannot rest at proper times, and above there is panting and gasping; entering the five viscera, there is distension, fullness, and blockage, and below there is undigested diarrhea, which in time becomes intestinal flux. Thus the throat governs the qi of Heaven, while the gullet governs the qi of Earth. Hence yang receives the qi of wind, yin receives the qi of dampness. Thus the yin qi travels upward from the feet to the head, then downward along the arms to the fingertips; the yang qi travels upward from the hands to the head, then downward to the feet. Hence it is said: in diseases of yang, the qi ascends to its limit and then descends; in diseases of yin, the qi descends to its limit and then ascends. Thus one injured by wind is affected first above; one injured by dampness is affected first below.

Huangdi said: When the spleen is diseased, the four limbs lose their function — why is this? Qibo said: The four limbs all receive their qi from the stomach, yet this qi cannot reach the channels directly; it must depend upon the spleen in order to be received. Now if the spleen is diseased and cannot circulate the fluids on behalf of the stomach, the four limbs cannot receive the qi of water and grain; the qi daily grows more depleted, the pathways of the vessels become unfree, and the sinews, bones, muscles, and flesh all lack the qi to be nourished and grow — hence they lose their function.

Huangdi said: Why does the spleen not govern a season of its own? Qibo said: The spleen belongs to earth and governs the center; it constantly nourishes the other four viscera throughout the four seasons, being entrusted with governance for eighteen days at the close of each season, and thus cannot alone preside over a single season. The spleen viscus constantly manifests the essence of the stomach's earth qi. Earth gives birth to the myriad things and takes its model from Heaven and Earth; hence its influence reaches upward and downward, to the head and to the feet, and it cannot be confined to governing one season alone.

Huangdi said: The spleen and stomach are joined only by a membrane, yet the spleen is able to circulate the stomach's fluids on its behalf — how is this? Qibo said: The Foot Greater Yin is one of the three yin; its vessel penetrates the stomach, belongs to the spleen, and connects with the gullet — hence Greater Yin circulates qi to the three yin on the stomach's behalf. Yang Brightness constitutes the exterior and is the sea of the five viscera and six bowels; it likewise circulates qi to the three yang. The viscera and bowels each receive qi from Yang Brightness by way of their respective channels; hence the spleen is able to circulate the stomach's fluids on its behalf. Should this fail, the four limbs cannot receive the qi of water and grain, growing daily more depleted; the pathways of yin become unfree, and the sinews, bones, muscles, and flesh lack the qi to be nourished and grow — hence they lose their function.

素问.title_en: Explanation of the Yang Brightness Vessel
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When the vessel of the Foot Yang Brightness is diseased, the patient comes to dislike people and fire, and startles in alarm on hearing the sound of wood struck, while the sound of bells and drums does not move him — why does the sound of wood alone startle him? I wish to hear the reason for this. Qibo replied: Yang Brightness is the vessel of the stomach, and the stomach belongs to earth; hence one who startles at the sound of wood does so because earth abhors wood. Huangdi said: Good. Why does he come to dislike fire? Qibo said: Yang Brightness governs the flesh, and its vessel is abundant in blood and qi; when evil qi lodges within it, heat results, and when the heat grows severe, the patient comes to dislike fire. Huangdi said: Why does he come to dislike people? Qibo said: When Yang Brightness suffers reversal, the patient pants and feels oppressed at heart; feeling oppressed at heart, he comes to dislike people. Huangdi said: In some cases the panting patient dies, in others the panting patient lives — why is this? Qibo said: When the reversed, counterflowing qi implicates the viscera, the patient dies; when it merely implicates the channels, the patient lives.

Huangdi said: Good. When the disease grows severe, the patient casts off his clothes and runs about, climbs to high places and sings, or goes for several days without eating, scaling walls and climbing onto roofs — reaching places that, in health, would be entirely beyond his ability. Why is it that in disease he is able to do what he ordinarily cannot? Qibo said: The four limbs are the root of all the yang; when yang is exuberant, the four limbs become replete, and being replete, the patient is able to climb to high places. Huangdi said: Why does he cast off his clothes and run about? Qibo said: Heat is exuberant throughout the body, and so he casts off his clothes and wishes to run. Huangdi said: Why does he rave, curse, and revile without regard for kin or stranger, all the while singing? Qibo said: When yang is exuberant, it causes a person to rave, curse, and revile without regard for kin or stranger, and to have no desire to eat; having no desire to eat, he runs about wildly.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Heat Disease
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Now, heat diseases are all of the category of cold damage; some patients recover, others die. Those who die all do so within six or seven days, while those who recover all take more than ten days. I do not understand the reason for this and wish to hear its explanation. Qibo replied: The Greater Yang is the chief of all the yang; its vessel connects with the Wind Mansion point, and therefore it governs the qi of all the yang channels. When a person is injured by cold, heat disease results; though the heat may be severe, the patient does not die. But one who is doubly stricken by cold, affected in both yin and yang at once, cannot escape death.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear what this looks like. Qibo said: On the first day of cold damage, the Greater Yang receives it, and so there is pain in the head and neck and stiffness of the lumbar spine. On the second day, Yang Brightness receives it; Yang Brightness governs the flesh, and its vessel flanks the nose and connects with the eyes, and so the body becomes hot, the eyes ache, the nose grows dry, and the patient cannot rest. On the third day, Lesser Yang receives it; Lesser Yang governs the gallbladder, and its vessel follows the flanks and connects with the ears, and so there is pain in the chest and flanks and deafness. When all three yang channels and their network vessels have received the disease, but it has not yet entered the viscera, the disease may be resolved by inducing sweat. On the fourth day, Greater Yin receives it; the Greater Yin vessel spreads through the stomach and connects with the gullet, and so there is abdominal fullness and dryness of the throat. On the fifth day, Lesser Yin receives it; the Lesser Yin vessel penetrates the kidney and connects with the lung, and links with the root of the tongue, and so the mouth parches, the tongue dries, and there is thirst. On the sixth day, Reverting Yin receives it; the Reverting Yin vessel follows the genitals and connects with the liver, and so there is vexation and fullness and contraction of the scrotum. When the three yin and three yang, and the five viscera and six bowels, have all received the disease, so that the nutritive and defensive qi no longer circulate and the five viscera no longer communicate, then the patient dies.

In one who is not doubly stricken by cold, on the seventh day the Greater Yang disease wanes and the headache somewhat improves; on the eighth day, the Yang Brightness disease wanes and the bodily heat somewhat improves; on the ninth day, the Lesser Yang disease wanes and hearing is faintly restored; on the tenth day, the Greater Yin disease wanes, the abdomen returns to its normal size, and the patient begins to think of food and drink; on the eleventh day, the Lesser Yin disease wanes, the thirst stops, the fullness subsides, the tongue's dryness ends, and the patient sneezes; on the twelfth day, the Reverting Yin disease wanes, the scrotum relaxes and the lower abdomen slightly descends, the pathogenic great qi departs entirely, and the disease resolves day by day. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: In treating it, one opens and frees the vessel corresponding to each viscus in turn, and the disease will wane and resolve day by day. In cases not yet past three days, one may resolve the disease by inducing sweat; in cases past three days, one may resolve it by purgation.

Huangdi said: When heat disease has already resolved, there are cases in which some residue lingers on — why is this? Qibo said: In all such cases of residue, the heat was still severe when the patient was forced to eat, and so a residue was left behind. In such cases the disease has already waned, yet heat still remains stored within; because the qi of the food clashes with it, the two heats combine, and so a residue remains. Huangdi said: Good. How is this residue treated? Qibo said: Examine whether it is deficiency or excess, and regulate what is rebellious and what is compliant, and the disease can certainly be made to resolve. Huangdi said: In heat disease, what should be forbidden? Qibo said: When heat disease has only somewhat improved, eating meat will cause relapse, and eating too much will leave a residue — these are the things to be forbidden.

Huangdi said: In cases doubly stricken by cold, what are the corresponding pulse and clinical signs? Qibo said: In double stricken by cold, on the first day of disease, both the Greater Yang and Lesser Yin are diseased together, causing headache, dry mouth, and vexatious fullness; on the second day, both Yang Brightness and Greater Yin are diseased together, causing abdominal fullness, bodily heat, disinclination to eat, and delirious speech; on the third day, both Lesser Yang and Reverting Yin are diseased together, causing deafness, contraction of the scrotum, and reversal, so that the patient can take neither water nor gruel and no longer recognizes those around him; on the sixth day he dies. Huangdi said: If the five viscera are already injured, the six bowels no longer communicate, and the nutritive and defensive qi no longer circulate, why does death then follow only after a further three days? Qibo said: Yang Brightness is the chief of the twelve channels and vessels, abundant in blood and qi; hence though the patient no longer recognizes others, it takes three more days for its qi to be entirely exhausted, and only then does death follow. In general, when cold damage disease transforms into warm disease: if it occurs before the Summer Solstice, it is called warm disease; if it occurs after the Summer Solstice, it is called summerheat disease. In summerheat disease, sweat should be allowed to flow out freely together with the heat, and should not be checked.

素问.title_en: Needling for Heat Disease
paragraphs_en: In liver heat disease, the urine first turns yellow, there is abdominal pain, excessive lying down, and bodily heat; when the heat contends within, the patient raves and starts in fright, the flanks are full and painful, the hands and feet are restless, and the patient cannot rest easy; the disease worsens on the days of the stems Geng and Xin, and profuse sweating occurs on the days of Jia and Yi; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Geng or Xin day. Needle the Foot Reverting Yin and Lesser Yang; when there is rebellion, the head aches dizzily, and the pulse draws upward, surging into the head. In heart heat disease, the patient first feels despondent, and only after some days does heat appear; when the heat contends, there is sudden heart pain, vexation and oppression, frequent retching, headache, a red face, and no sweating; the disease worsens on Ren and Gui days, and profuse sweating occurs on Bing and Ding days; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Ren or Gui day. Needle the Hand Lesser Yin and Greater Yang. In spleen heat disease, the head first feels heavy and the cheeks ache, there is vexation of the heart, a greenish complexion, a desire to retch, and bodily heat; when the heat contends, there is lumbar pain that prevents bending forward or backward, abdominal fullness and diarrhea, and pain in both sides of the jaw; the disease worsens on Jia and Yi days, and profuse sweating occurs on Wu and Ji days; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Jia or Yi day. Needle the Foot Greater Yin and Yang Brightness. In lung heat disease, the patient first shivers as if drenched, the fine body hairs stand on end, there is aversion to wind and cold, a yellow coating on the tongue, and bodily heat; when the heat contends, there is panting and coughing, pain that travels through the chest and back so the patient cannot take a deep breath, unbearable headache, and sweating together with chill; the disease worsens on Bing and Ding days, and profuse sweating occurs on Geng and Xin days; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Bing or Ding day. Needle the Hand Greater Yin and Yang Brightness until blood the size of a large bean emerges, and the disease resolves at once. In kidney heat disease, the patient first suffers lumbar pain and aching of the shins, distressing thirst with frequent drinking, and bodily heat; when the heat contends, there is pain and stiffness of the neck, cold and aching of the shins, heat in the soles of the feet, and disinclination to speak; when there is rebellion, the neck aches with a dizzy, wavering sensation; the disease worsens on Wu and Ji days, and profuse sweating occurs on Ren and Gui days; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Wu or Ji day. Needle the Foot Lesser Yin and Greater Yang. In every case where sweating is due, it occurs upon the day that overcomes the disease's own phase. In liver heat disease, redness first appears on the left cheek; in heart heat disease, redness first appears on the forehead; in spleen heat disease, redness first appears on the nose; in lung heat disease, redness first appears on the right cheek; in kidney heat disease, redness first appears on the chin. Even before the disease has manifested, if this redness is seen, one should needle at once — this is called treating the disease before it arises. When heat disease arises from the region corresponding to its own phase, it resolves by the appointed time; when one needles contrary to this, it resolves only after three cycles; and where the rebellion is compounded, the patient dies. In every case where sweating is due, it comes forth profusely on the day that overcomes the disease's own phase. In treating heat disease generally, give the patient cold water to drink before needling, dress him in cool clothing, and keep him in a cool place, stopping once the body has cooled.

When heat disease begins with pain in the chest and flanks and restlessness of the hands and feet, needle the Foot Lesser Yang and supplement the Foot Greater Yin; in severe cases, apply the fifty-nine-point needling. When heat disease begins with pain in the hand and arm, needle the Hand Yang Brightness and Greater Yin, and stop when sweating occurs. When heat disease begins in the head, needle the Greater Yang points of the neck, and stop when sweating occurs. When heat disease begins in the foot and shin, needle the Foot Yang Brightness, and stop when sweating occurs. When heat disease begins with heaviness of the body and aching of the bones, deafness, and a fondness for closing the eyes to sleep, needle the Foot Lesser Yin; in severe cases, apply the fifty-nine-point needling. When heat disease begins with dizziness and clouded vision together with heat, and fullness of the chest and flanks, needle the Foot Lesser Yin and Lesser Yang. When the color of the Greater Yang vessel glows at the cheekbone, this is heat disease; if the color has not yet spread to meet the adjoining region, one may say sweat will soon be obtained, and one need only await the proper time for it to resolve. But if this color appears contending together with the color of the Reverting Yin vessel, the time of death is no more than three days away, for the heat disease has penetrated inward to connect with the kidney — this being the color of the Lesser Yang vessel. When the color of the Lesser Yang vessel glows in front of the cheek, this is heat disease; if the color has not yet spread to meet the adjoining region, one may say sweat will soon be obtained, and one need only await the proper time for it to resolve; but if this color appears contending together with the color of the Lesser Yin vessel, the time of death is no more than three days away.

In heat disease, among the qi points: the space below the third vertebra governs heat in the chest; the space below the fourth vertebra governs heat in the diaphragm region; the space below the fifth vertebra governs heat of the liver; the space below the sixth vertebra governs heat of the spleen; the space below the seventh vertebra governs heat of the kidney; and the point for the telltale glow lies at the sacrum. The depression above the neck at the third vertebra corresponds to the center. Where the glow runs from the cheek up counter to the cheekbone, it signifies a great abdominal mass; where it appears below at the jaw, it signifies abdominal fullness; where it appears behind the cheekbone, it signifies pain in the flanks; where it appears on the upper cheek, it signifies involvement above the diaphragm.

素问.title_en: Discourse Evaluating Heat Disease
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: There is a disease of warmth in which, whenever sweat comes out, the fever promptly returns, the pulse remains agitated and rapid without abating along with the sweat, and the patient raves and cannot eat — what is this disease called? Qibo replied: This disease is called yin-yang crossing; crossing means death. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the explanation. Qibo said: The reason a person sweats is that sweat is always generated from grain, and grain in turn generates essence. Now when the evil qi contends with the essence within the bones and flesh and sweat is thereby obtained, this signifies that the evil has retreated and the essence has prevailed; when the essence prevails, the patient ought to be able to eat and the fever ought not to return. A return of fever indicates the evil qi; sweat indicates the essential qi. Now if sweat comes out and the fever promptly returns, this signifies that the evil has prevailed. Inability to eat means the essence has nothing to replenish it. When the disease lingers on in this way, the patient's life may be overturned in an instant. Moreover, the Discourse on Heat states: if sweat comes out while the pulse remains agitated and exuberant, the patient dies. Now since the pulse fails to correspond to the sweating, this shows the patient cannot overcome the disease, and death is certain. Raving speech signifies the loss of the will; loss of the will means death. Since here we see three signs of death and not a single sign of life, even should the patient seem to improve, he will certainly die.

Huangdi said: There is a disease in which the body is hot, sweat comes out, and there is vexation and fullness, yet the vexation and fullness are not relieved by the sweating — what disease is this? Qibo said: Sweating together with bodily heat is due to wind; sweating with vexation and fullness that is not relieved is due to reversal — this disease is called wind reversal. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the whole of it. Qibo said: The Greater Yang governs the qi and therefore receives the evil first; the Lesser Yin stands with it in exterior-interior relation, and when it encounters this heat, it follows the Greater Yang's qi upward, and this following-upward constitutes the reversal. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: Needle both the exterior and interior channels, and give the patient decoctions to drink.

Huangdi said: What manner of disease is taxation-wind? Qibo said: Taxation-wind, as a rule, lodges beneath the lung; as a disease it causes stiffness in the upper back and dimness of vision, with spittle coming forth like mucus, aversion to wind, and shivering with cold — this is the disease of taxation-wind. Huangdi said: How is it treated? Qibo said: Treatment aims to restore the ability to bend and stretch. In one whose Greater Yang still draws upon sufficient essence, it resolves in three days; in a person of middle years, in five days; in one deficient in essence, in seven days, when the patient coughs up greenish-yellow mucus resembling pus, as large as a pellet, coming out through the mouth or nose. If it fails to come out, it will injure the lung, and injury to the lung brings death.

Huangdi said: There is a disease called kidney wind, in which the face becomes puffy and swollen, and speech is impaired — may this be treated with needling? Qibo said: When it is due to deficiency, needling is not appropriate; if one needles when it is not appropriate, then after five days the pathogenic qi is certain to arrive in force. Huangdi said: What is it like when it arrives? Qibo said: When it arrives, the patient certainly suffers shortness of breath and intermittent fever, the fever rising from the chest and back up to the head, with sweating, hot hands, dry mouth, bitter and distressing thirst, yellow urine, swelling beneath the eyes, rumbling in the abdomen, heaviness of the body making walking difficult, cessation of the menses, vexation with inability to eat, and inability to lie flat on the back — for lying flat brings on coughing. This disease is called wind water, and its treatment is discussed among the needling methods. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the explanation. Qibo said: Wherever evil qi gathers, the correct qi there is certainly deficient; when yin is deficient, yang is bound to gather there — hence the shortness of breath, intermittent fever, and sweating. Yellow urine indicates heat within the lower abdomen. Inability to lie flat indicates disharmony within the stomach; when the patient does lie flat, the coughing grows severe, because the qi presses upward upon the lung. In all cases of water qi, a slight swelling appears first beneath the eyes. Huangdi said: On what basis is this said? Qibo said: Water belongs to yin, and the region beneath the eyes likewise belongs to yin; the abdomen is where utmost yin resides. Hence when water lodges in the abdomen, it necessarily causes swelling beneath the eyes as well. When the true qi rebels upward, the mouth turns bitter and the tongue dry, and the patient cannot lie flat; when he does lie flat, he coughs up clear water. In all water diseases, the patient therefore cannot rest lying down; if he does lie down, he startles, and starting brings on severe coughing. Rumbling in the abdomen indicates the disease is rooted in the stomach. When it presses upon the spleen, there is vexation and inability to eat; when food fails to descend, this is obstruction at the stomach duct. Heaviness of the body making walking difficult is because the stomach vessel runs through the feet. Cessation of the menses is due to closure of the uterine vessel; the uterine vessel belongs to the heart and connects with the interior of the uterus. Now, since the qi presses upward upon the lung, the heart qi cannot communicate downward, and so the menses fail to come. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Discourse on Counterflow Regulation
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When a person's body is not unusually warm and not unusually hot, yet the patient experiences heat with vexation and fullness — why is this? Qibo replied: This is because the yin qi is diminished while the yang qi prevails; hence there is heat with vexation and fullness.

Huangdi said: When a person's clothing is not cold and there is no cold qi within, yet cold arises from within — why is this? Qibo said: This is a person with abundant impediment qi; the yang qi is diminished and the yin qi abundant, and so the body feels as cold as if just emerged from water.

Huangdi said: There are people whose four limbs are hot, and when they encounter wind and cold, the limbs feel as if scorched by fire — why is this? Qibo said: In such a person, the yin qi is deficient and the yang qi exuberant. The four limbs belong to yang; when yang meets yang, while the yin qi is scant and deficient, the scant water cannot extinguish the exuberant fire, so that yang alone governs. Where yang alone governs, it cannot properly generate and nurture growth — it only prevails alone until it exhausts itself. When such a person encounters wind and the limbs feel as if scorched by fire, this signifies that his flesh is being consumed away.

Huangdi said: There are people whose bodies are cold, whom neither hot broths nor fire can warm, nor thick clothing make warm, and yet they do not shiver from the cold — what disease is this? Qibo said: In such a person, the kidney qi has habitually prevailed, given over to the affairs of water, so that the Greater Yang qi has declined, and the kidney's fat has withered and ceased to grow; a single portion of water cannot overcome two portions of fire. The kidney belongs to water and generates the bones; when the kidney fails to generate, the marrow cannot be filled, and so the cold penetrates severely even into the bones. The reason such a person does not shiver is that the liver is one yang and the heart is a second yang, while the kidney is a solitary viscus; one portion of water cannot overcome two portions of fire, and so the patient cannot shiver. This disease is called bone impediment, and such a person is bound to suffer contracture of the joints.

Huangdi said: There are people whose flesh feels numb and unresponsive, so that even wrapped close in padded clothing it remains numb — what illness is this called? Qibo said: This is when the nutritive qi is deficient while the defensive qi is excess. When the nutritive qi is deficient, there is numbness; when the defensive qi is deficient, there is loss of function. When both nutritive and defensive qi are deficient, there is both numbness and loss of function, though the flesh appears outwardly unchanged. When the person's body and will no longer belong to one another, this is called death.

Huangdi said: There are people with counterflow qi who cannot lie down and whose breathing makes a sound; there are those who cannot lie down yet whose breathing makes no sound; there are those whose rising and resting are as usual yet whose breathing makes a sound; there are those who can lie down and walk about yet pant; there are those who cannot lie down and cannot walk yet pant; and there are those who cannot lie down, and when they do lie down, they pant. What viscus in each case brings this about? I wish to hear the explanation. Qibo said: Those who cannot lie down and whose breathing makes a sound suffer from counterflow of the Yang Brightness. The three yang channels of the foot normally flow downward; now, in counterflow, they flow upward, and so the breath makes a sound. Yang Brightness is the vessel of the stomach, and the stomach is the sea of the six bowels, its qi likewise flowing downward normally; when Yang Brightness rebels and cannot follow its proper path, the patient cannot lie down. As the Lower Canon states: when the stomach is not in harmony, sleep is not peaceful — this is what is meant. As for those whose rising and resting are as usual yet whose breathing makes a sound, this is counterflow in the network vessels of the lung; the network vessels cannot follow the channels in their upward and downward movement, and so the qi lingers in the channel and fails to move on; disease of the network vessels affects a person only mildly, and so rising and resting remain as usual even while the breath makes a sound. As for those who cannot lie down, and who pant when they do lie down, this is due to a visitation of water qi; water flows along with the body fluids, and the kidney is the viscus of water, governing the body fluids, and thus governing both the ability to lie down and the occurrence of panting. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Treatise on Malaria
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: All forms of malaria arise from wind, yet their accumulation and onset occur at fixed times — why is this so? Qibo replied: When malaria first breaks out, it begins in the fine body hairs; the patient stretches and yawns, then the attack comes on, with shivering cold and chattering of the jaw, and pain throughout the lower back and spine. When the cold passes, heat rises both within and without, the head aches as though splitting, and the patient thirsts for cold drink. Huangdi asked: What qi brings this about? I wish to hear the explanation. Qibo said: Yin and yang contend with one another above and below, deficiency and excess alternate, and yin and yang displace each other.
