If summer has the domination of bleak desolation, congealing and biting cold, then at unseasonable times there will be the retaliation of murky dust and heavy rain. Its calamity is in the south, its viscus is the heart, its disease internally lodges in the chest and flanks, and externally is in the channels and vessels. When earth is deficient, if the four cardinal-adjacent directions have the transformation of dusty clouds and moistening dampness, then spring will have the governance of whistling branches and bursting-open buds. If the four cardinal-adjacent directions give rise to the mutation of shaking, breaking, and whirling turbulence, then autumn will have the retaliation of stern killing and prolonged drenching rain. Its calamity is in the four cardinal-adjacent directions, its viscus is the spleen, its disease internally lodges in the heart and abdomen, and externally is in the flesh and four limbs. When metal is deficient, if summer has the command of radiant brightness and stifling steaminess, then winter will have the response of severe congealing cold and stern order. If summer has the mutation of scorching, blazing fire, then autumn will have the retaliation of ice, hail, frost, and snow. Its calamity is in the west, its viscus is the lung, its disease internally lodges in the chest, flanks, shoulders, and back, and externally is in the skin and body hair. When water is deficient, if the four cardinal-adjacent directions have the transformation of rushing moisture and dusty clouds, then at unseasonable times there will be the response of mild wind and generative growth. If the four cardinal-adjacent directions give rise to the mutation of murky dust and sudden downpour, then at unseasonable times there will be the retaliation of whirling turbulence and shaking, breaking winds. Its calamity is in the north, its viscus is the kidney, its disease internally lodges in the lower back, spine, bones, and marrow, and externally is in the valleys of the flesh and the calves and knees. Now the governance of the five movements is like a steelyard balance: what is high is pressed down, what is low is raised up; what transforms is met with response, what mutates is met with retaliation. This is the principle of generation, growth, transformation, ripening, gathering, and storing — the constancy of qi. When this constancy is lost, heaven and earth become blocked on all four sides. Hence it is said: the motion and stillness of heaven and earth are governed by spirit-brightness as their guiding thread; the going and returning of yin and yang are made manifest in their portents by cold and heat — this is what is meant.

Huangdi said: What you, Master, have said of the mutations of the five qi and their correspondence to the four seasons may be called thorough indeed. But when the movement of qi becomes disordered, arising upon chance encounter, breaking out without regular timing, and calamity suddenly converges — how can one anticipate this? Qibo said: Now the movement and mutation of qi is indeed not constantly present, and its virtue, transformation, governance, command, calamity, and mutation each have their own distinct signs. Huangdi said: What does this mean? Qibo said: The east generates wind, wind generates wood; its virtue is to spread harmony, its transformation is to generate flourishing growth, its governance is to loosen and open, its command is wind, its mutation is shaking and bursting-forth, and its calamity is scattering and shedding. The south generates heat, heat generates fire; its virtue is to make manifest and display, its transformation is to make luxuriant and lush, its governance is to brighten and shine, its command is heat, its mutation is melting and scorching, and its calamity is burning and consuming. The center generates dampness, dampness generates earth; its virtue is to steam with moisture, its transformation is to complete abundantly, its governance is to settle and quiet, its command is dampness, its mutation is sudden downpour, and its calamity is prolonged rain and collapse. The west generates dryness, dryness generates metal; its virtue is to clear and cleanse, its transformation is to tighten and contract, its governance is to be firm and incisive, its command is dryness, its mutation is stern killing, and its calamity is the shedding of green foliage. The north generates cold, cold generates water; its virtue is bleak chill, its transformation is clear stillness, its governance is congealing sternness, its command is cold, its mutation is biting severity, and its calamity is ice, snow, frost, and hail. Therefore, in observing its movements, there is virtue and there is transformation, there is governance and there is command, there is mutation and there is calamity; living things follow from these, and man responds to them.

Huangdi said: You, Master, have spoken of the yearly signs — their deficiency and excess — corresponding above to the five planets. Now as for virtue, transformation, governance, command, calamity, and change, these occur when not constant; when they move suddenly, do these too bring about mutation in the stars? Qibo said: The stars proceed by carrying forward the mandate of heaven, and therefore have no reckless movement — there is nothing that goes without response. What moves suddenly is a mutation in the exchange of qi, and to this the stars do not respond. Hence it is said: they respond to the constant but do not respond to the sudden — this is what is meant. Huangdi said: How do they respond? Qibo said: Each follows its own transformation of qi. Huangdi said: What of the slowness or swiftness, the counterflow or compliant motion, of their movement? Qibo said: When a planet lingers long upon its path, moving contrary and staying fixed while small, this is called 'inspecting what is below.' When it departs along its path, departs and swiftly returns, or curves and passes beyond, this is called 'inspecting an oversight or transgression.' When it lingers long and circles about, now separating, now drawing close, this is called 'deliberating over calamity and virtue.' When the response is near, the star appears small; when the response is distant, it appears large. When it is radiant and larger than usual by half again, its transformation is severe; when it is larger than usual by twice, its calamity is imminent. When it is smaller than usual by half, its transformation diminishes; when it is smaller than usual by twice, this is called 'looking down and inspecting,' scrutinizing the transgressions and virtues of what lies below. Where there is virtue, it brings blessing; where there is transgression, it metes out chastisement. Hence, as for the appearance of these images: when high and distant, the star appears small; when low and near, it appears large. Therefore when large, joy and anger are close at hand; when small, fortune and misfortune are distant. When the yearly movement is in excess, then the corresponding star strays northward beyond its course; when the movement and qi are in mutual accord, then each proceeds along its proper path. Thus when the yearly movement is in excess, the star that is feared loses its normal color and takes on that of its mother; when it is deficient, its color takes on that of what it cannot overcome. For the resembling signs, one must be watchful and alert, for none can know their subtlety; anxiously must one attend to what is fitting — who can judge which is auspicious? Reckless movement without proof serves only to display an ominous warning to lords and kings. Huangdi said: How does the response to calamity manifest? Qibo said: This too follows each star's transformation. Thus in the arrival of the season there is flourishing and decline; in encroachment and violation there is counterflow and compliance; in lingering and staying there is more and less; in the appearance of forms there is good and ill; among the lodges there is dominance and subordination; and in the verifying responses there is fortune and misfortune. Huangdi said: What is meant by 'good and ill'? Qibo said: There is joy and there is anger, there is grief and there is mourning, there is moistness and there is dryness — these are the constants of the celestial images, and one must carefully observe them. Huangdi said: Do these six differ according to whether the star is high or low? Qibo said: Though the image appears high or low, its response is one and the same; therefore man likewise responds to it.

Huangdi said: Good. What of the motion, stillness, diminishment, and increase of virtue, transformation, governance, and command? Qibo said: Now virtue, transformation, governance, command, calamity, and mutation cannot be added one upon another. Domination and retaliation, flourishing and decline, cannot exceed one another in degree. Going and coming, smallness and largeness, cannot surpass one another. Their function of rising and descending, they cannot be without one another. Each simply follows its own movement and then returns to balance. Huangdi said: How does disease arise from this? Qibo said: Virtue and transformation are the auspicious signs of qi; governance and command are the manifest patterns of qi; mutation and change are the guiding thread of retaliation; calamity and misfortune are the beginning of harm. When the qi mutually overcome each other in balance, there is harmony; when they fail to overcome each other properly, there is disease; and when one is doubly affected by pathogenic influence, the disease becomes severe. Huangdi said: Good. This may be called a discourse of essential brilliance, the work of a great sage, proclaiming and illuminating the great Dao, penetrating to what is inexhaustible and reaching to what is boundless. I have heard it said: one who speaks well of heaven must find correspondence in man; one who speaks well of antiquity must find verification in the present; one who speaks well of qi must find it made manifest in living things; one who speaks well of correspondence shares in the transformations of heaven and earth; one who speaks well of transformation and mutation penetrates the principles of spirit-brightness. Were it not for you, Master, who could speak of the utmost Dao? Thereupon he selected an auspicious day and stored this discourse in the Chamber of the Numinous, reading it every morning; he named it 'Mutations in the Exchange of Qi,' and dared not open it except after fasting and purification, so carefully was it to be transmitted.

素问.title_en: Great Treatise on the Five Constant Governances
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: The great void is vast and empty; the five movements circulate and press upon one another, waning and waxing unequally, diminishing and increasing in mutual succession. I wish to hear how the balanced qi is named, and how it is classified. Qibo replied: How illuminating is this question! Wood, in balance, is called Spreading Harmony; fire is called Ascending Brightness; earth is called Comprehensive Transformation; metal is called Discerning Balance; water is called Tranquil Compliance. Huangdi said: What of when they are insufficient? Qibo said: Wood, when insufficient, is called Diminished Harmony; fire is called Suppressed Brightness; earth is called Debased Oversight; metal is called Compliant Change; water is called Dried-up Flow. Huangdi said: What is meant by excess? Qibo said: Wood, when excessive, is called Bursting Forth Life; fire is called Blazing Brilliance; earth is called Abundant Elevation; metal is called Firm Completion; water is called Overflowing Abundance. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the signs of these three levels of qi in their categories. Qibo said: How thorough is this question! In the category of Spreading Harmony, the virtue of wood circulates everywhere; yang unfolds and yin spreads, and the five transformations are proclaimed in balance. Its qi is upright; its nature is compliant; its function is bending and straightening; its transformation is engendering flourishing growth; its class is grasses and trees; its governance is outward release; its climatic sign is mild warmth; its seasonal command is wind; its viscus is the liver — the liver fears clear coolness; its ruling orifice is the eye; its grain is hemp; its fruit is the plum; its seed-body is the pit; its correspondence is spring; its creatures are the furred kind; its domestic animal is the dog; its color is blue-green; its nourishment is the sinews; its disease is urgent tension and distension of the flanks; its flavor is sour; its note is jue; its substance is hard at the core; its number is eight. In the category of Ascending Brightness, true yang governs; its virtue is bestowed everywhere and universally, and the five transformations are evenly balanced. Its qi is lofty; its nature is swift; its function is scorching and blazing; its transformation is luxuriant flourishing; its class is fire; its governance is bright radiance; its climatic sign is blazing heat; its seasonal command is heat; its viscus is the heart — the heart fears cold; its ruling orifice is the tongue; its grain is wheat; its fruit is the apricot; its seed-body is net-veined; its correspondence is summer; its creatures are the feathered kind; its domestic animal is the horse; its color is red; its nourishment is the blood; its disease is twitching and spasm; its flavor is bitter; its note is zhi; its substance is the pulse-vessel; its number is seven. In the category of Comprehensive Transformation, its qi harmonizes with heaven's blessing; its virtue flows through the governance of all four others, and the five transformations are equally cultivated. Its qi is level; its nature is compliant; its function acts on the high and the low alike; its transformation is fullness and abundance; its class is earth; its governance is calm stillness; its climatic sign is moist, steaming heat; its seasonal command is dampness; its viscus is the spleen — the spleen fears wind; its ruling orifice is the mouth; its grain is millet; its fruit is the jujube; its seed-body is flesh; its correspondence is late summer; its creatures are the bare-skinned kind; its domestic animal is the ox; its color is yellow; its nourishment is the flesh; its disease is blockage and fullness; its flavor is sweet; its note is gong; its substance is the flesh; its number is five. In the category of Discerning Balance, it gathers in without contention and kills without transgression; the five transformations are proclaimed clearly. Its qi is pure; its nature is firm; its function is scattering and shedding; its transformation is hardening and constraining; its class is metal; its governance is forceful sternness; its climatic sign is clear sharpness; its seasonal command is dryness; its viscus is the lung — the lung fears heat; its ruling orifice is the nose; its grain is rice; its fruit is the peach; its seed-body is the husk; its correspondence is autumn; its creatures are the shelled kind; its domestic animal is the fowl; its color is white; its nourishment is the skin and body hair; its disease is coughing; its flavor is pungent; its note is shang; its substance is hard on the outside; its number is nine. In the category of Tranquil Compliance, it stores away without causing harm, and governs by favoring descent; the five transformations are all put in order. Its qi is clear; its nature is downward; its function is to irrigate abundantly; its transformation is congealing and hardening; its class is water; its governance is flowing and spreading; its climatic sign is congealing sternness; its seasonal command is cold; its viscus is the kidney — the kidney fears dampness; its ruling orifice is the two lower orifices; its grain is beans; its fruit is the chestnut; its seed-body is moist; its correspondence is winter; its creatures are the scaled kind; its domestic animal is the pig; its color is black; its nourishment is the bones and marrow; its disease is reversal; its flavor is salty; its note is yu; its substance is moist; its number is six. Therefore: engender life without killing it; foster growth without punishing it; transform without controlling it; gather in without harming it; store away without suppressing it — this is called the balanced qi.

In the category of Diminished Harmony, this is called 'the overcoming of engendering.' The qi of engendering fails to govern, so the transforming qi of earth rises up; the qi of growth settles into its own balance, while the command of harvesting arrives early. Cool rains fall in their season; wind and clouds arise together. Grasses and trees flourish late, their blue-green stalks wither and shed; yet things that bloom still bear fruit, the skin and flesh filling within. Its qi is constrained; its function is gathering inward; its pathological movement is cramping, twisting, and slackened contraction; its outbreak brings fright and alarm. Its viscus is the liver; its fruits are the jujube and plum; its seed-bodies are pits and husks; its grains are millet and rice; its flavors are sour and pungent; its colors are white and blue-green; its domestic animals are the dog and fowl; its creatures are the furred and shelled kinds; its characteristic weather is fog, dew, and bleak chill; its notes are jue and shang; its disease is trembling, unsteadiness, and anxious fear, following the transformation of metal. When the minor jue coincides with the intercalated shang, when the ascending jue matches the true jue, and when the ascending shang matches the true shang, its disease is limb paralysis, welling-abscess, swelling, and sores, with sweet-craving parasites — evil has injured the liver. When the ascending gong matches the true gong, and withering desolation sets in, then blazing heat surges and boils over; the calamity falls at the third number — this is what is called the 'revenge.' Its governing creatures are flying insects, wood-borers, maggots, and pheasants, and it manifests as thunder and lightning. In the category of Suppressed Brightness, this is called 'the overcoming of growth.' The qi of growth fails to spread; the storing qi of water instead prevails; the harvesting qi governs on its own; the transforming command achieves balance. Cold and clear chill arise repeatedly; the command of summer heat grows thin. Sustained by transformation, things are engendered yet fail to grow; they reach completion while still immature, and by the time transformation arrives they have already aged. Yang qi is bent and suppressed; hibernating creatures retreat into dormancy early. Its qi is stagnant; its function is sudden and violent; its pathological movement alternates between manifest and hidden, ever-changing; its outbreak brings pain. Its viscus is the heart; its fruits are the chestnut and peach; its seed-bodies are net-veined and moist; its grains are beans and rice; its flavors are bitter and salty; its colors are black and vermilion; its domestic animals are the horse and pig; its creatures are the feathered and scaled kinds; its characteristic weather is ice, snow, frost, and cold; its notes are zhi and yu; its disease is confusion, delusion, sorrow, and forgetfulness, following the transformation of water. When the minor zhi matches the minor yu, and the ascending shang matches the true shang, evil has injured the heart. When congealing bleakness and biting cold prevail, torrential rains and prolonged downpours follow; the calamity falls at the ninth number; its governing signs are sudden cloudbursts, startling thunder and lightning, and deep overcast with excessive rain. In the category of Debased Oversight, this is called 'diminished transformation.' The transforming qi fails to issue its command; the governance of engendering alone flourishes; the qi of growth is orderly, the rains fall irregularly, and the harvesting qi is balanced. Wind and cold arise together; grasses and trees flourish beautifully, blooming but not bearing fruit, and what they complete is mere chaff. Its qi is dispersed; its function is quiet stillness; its pathological movement is ulceration, welling swelling, splitting, and abscess-collapse; its outbreak is dampness and stagnation. Its viscus is the spleen; its fruits are the plum and chestnut; its seed-bodies are moist and pitted; its grains are beans and hemp; its flavors are sour and sweet; its colors are blue-green and yellow; its domestic animals are the ox and dog; its creatures are the bare-skinned and furred kinds; its characteristic weather is whirling gusts and violent gales; its notes are gong and jue; its disease is retention, fullness, and blockage, following the transformation of wood. When the minor gong matches the minor jue, when the ascending gong matches the true gong, and the ascending jue matches the true jue, its disease is undigested-food diarrhea — evil has injured the spleen. When shaking gales and whirling winds prevail, the blue-green foliage withers and scatters; its calamity falls at the four cardinal corners; its governing sign is destruction by tigers and wolves; clear metal qi then takes effect, and the governance of engendering is humbled. In the category of Compliant Change, this is called 'broken harvest.' The harvesting qi falls behind; the engendering qi rises up; the qi of growth and transformation combine their virtues; the governance of fire is proclaimed, and the multitude of creatures thereby flourish. Its qi is expansive; its function is agitated and urgent; its pathological movement is clanging spasm, blockage, and fainting reversal; its outbreak is coughing and panting. Its viscus is the lung; its fruits are the plum and apricot; its seed-bodies are husk and net-fiber; its grains are hemp and wheat; its flavors are bitter and pungent; its colors are white and vermilion; its domestic animals are the fowl and sheep; its creatures are the shelled and feathered kinds; its characteristic weather is bright radiance and searing heat; its notes are shang and zhi; its disease is sneezing, coughing, and nasal congestion with bleeding, following the transformation of fire. When the minor shang matches the minor zhi, when the ascending shang matches the true shang, and the ascending jue matches the true jue, evil has injured the lung. When blazing brilliance grows fierce and intense, ice, snow, frost, and hail follow; the calamity falls at the seventh number; its governing creatures are the scaled and hibernating kinds, pigs and rats. The seasonal qi arrives early, and great cold is thereby engendered. In the category of Dried-up Flow, this is called 'the reversal of yang.' The storing command fails to be raised; the transforming qi of earth flourishes; the growth qi is proclaimed and spread; hibernating creatures fail to retreat into dormancy. The soil is moist yet the springs diminish; grasses and trees grow luxuriant in their branches, blooming in full abundance. Its qi is stagnant; its function is seeping and draining; its pathological movement is hardening and blockage; its outbreak is dryness and desiccation. Its viscus is the kidney; its fruits are the jujube and apricot; its seed-bodies are moist flesh; its grains are broomcorn and millet; its flavors are sweet and salty; its colors are yellow and black; its domestic animals are the pig and ox; its creatures are the scaled and bare-skinned kinds; its characteristic weather is murky dust and gloom-shrouded haze; its notes are yu and gong; its disease is wilting, reversal, and hardened stool, following the transformation of earth. When the minor yu matches the minor gong, and the ascending gong matches the true gong, its disease is urinary blockage — evil has injured the kidney. When dust-laden gloom brings sudden downpours, shaking, uprooting gales follow; the calamity falls at the first number; its governing creatures are conspicuous furred ones, foxes and raccoon-dogs. Change occurs without proper storage; thus the counter-qi rides upon the vulnerable moment and moves, arriving unsummoned, violent and tyrannical, devoid of virtue, and disaster in turn befalls it. When the deficiency is slight, the revenge is slight; when severe, the revenge is severe — this is the constancy of qi.

In the category of Bursting Forth Life, this is called 'opening and unfurling.' The soil is loosened and drained; the blue-green qi of wood reaches through; harmonious yang spreads its transforming influence, and yin qi follows along. The qi of engendering is pure and transforming, and the myriad things thereby flourish. Its transformation is engendering; its qi is lush; its governance is dispersal; its command is unfolding and stretching; its pathological movement is dizziness, vertigo, and disorders of the vertex; its virtue manifests as rustling wind and the opening and splitting of buds; its calamitous change is shaking, uprooting, destructive gales. Its grains are hemp and rice; its domestic animals are the fowl and dog; its fruits are the plum and peach; its colors are blue-green, yellow, and white; its flavors are sour, sweet, and pungent; its season-symbol is spring; its channels are the foot jueyin and shaoyang; its viscera are the liver and spleen; its creatures are the furred and shelled kinds; its substances are hard at the core and hard on the outside; its disease is anger. When the greater jue matches the ascending shang, and when the ascending zhi prevails so that its qi turns rebellious, its disease is vomiting and diarrhea. If wood fails to attend to its virtue, then the harvesting qi of metal takes revenge: the autumn qi grows forceful and keen, and in severe cases turns to killing severity; clear metal qi arrives in full force, grasses and trees wither and fall, and evil then injures the liver. In the category of Blazing Brilliance, this is called 'luxuriant flourishing.' Yin qi transforms within, yang qi flourishes without; blazing summer heat carries out its transformation, and things thereby prosper. Its transformation is growth; its qi is lofty; its governance is movement; its command is resounding and manifest; its pathological movement is scorching heat and reckless disturbance; its virtue manifests as warmth, heat, and steaming humidity; its calamitous change is fierce blazing and seething boil-over. Its grains are wheat and beans; its domestic animals are the sheep and pig; its fruits are the apricot and chestnut; its colors are red, white, and black; its flavors are bitter, pungent, and salty; its season-symbol is summer; its channels are the hand shaoyin and taiyang, and the hand jueyin and shaoyang; its viscera are the heart and lung; its creatures are the feathered and scaled kinds; its substances are the pulse-vessel and moist flesh; its disease is manic laughter, malarial disorders, sores and ulcers, blood flowing freely, wild delirium, and reddened eyes. When the ascending yu matches the true zhi, its harvest phase proceeds evenly, yet its disease is tetanic rigidity, for when the ascending zhi prevails the harvesting qi of metal lags behind. When its governance grows violently intense, the storing qi of water takes revenge, and congealing bleakness appears out of season; in severe cases rain, frost, and hail bring biting cold, and evil injures the heart. In the category of Abundant Elevation, this is called 'broad transformation.' Its generous virtue is pure and still; it follows growth so as to become full; the utmost yin is replete within, and things are transformed to fullness and completion. Smoke-like haze and dust hang thick and murky, appearing over the deep soil; heavy rains fall in season; damp qi thereby takes effect, and the governance of dryness is held in check. Its transformation is fullness; its qi is abundant; its governance is stillness; its command is all-encompassing; its pathological movement is dampness accumulating and pooling; its virtue manifests as softness, moistness, and heavy sodden wetness; its calamitous change is shock, sudden gales, and collapse. Its grains are millet and hemp; its domestic animals are the ox and dog; its fruits are the jujube and plum; its colors are yellow, black, and blue-green; its flavors are sweet, salty, and sour; its season-symbol is late summer; its channels are the foot taiyin and yangming; its viscera are the spleen and kidney; its creatures are the bare-skinned and furred kinds; its substances are the flesh and the pit; its disease is abdominal fullness and inability to raise the four limbs. When great winds arrive suddenly, evil injures the spleen. In the category of Firm Completion, this is called 'gathering and contracting.' Heaven's qi is pure; earth's qi is bright; yang qi follows along; yin governs the transformation; dryness carries out its governance, and things are thereby brought to completion. The harvesting qi spreads abundantly, yet the transformation of growth does not reach its end. Its transformation is completion; its qi is pared and sharp; its governance is stern; its command is keen and cutting; its pathological movement is sudden fractures, sores, and chronic ailments; its virtue manifests as fog, dew, and rustling wind; its calamitous change is killing severity and withering desolation. Its grains are rice and broomcorn millet; its domestic animals are the fowl and horse; its fruits are the peach and apricot; its colors are white, blue-green, and vermilion; its flavors are pungent, sour, and bitter; its season-symbol is autumn; its channels are the hand taiyin and yangming; its viscera are the lung and liver; its creatures are the shelled and feathered kinds; its substances are shell and net-fiber; its disease is panting, wheezing, chest oppression, and having to sit upright to breathe. When the ascending zhi matches the true shang, its engendering phase proceeds evenly, yet its disease is coughing. When its governance changes violently, even the great trees fail to flourish and the tender, brittle growth scorches at the tip; the growth qi of fire then comes to the rescue — a great fire spreads, searing heat arrives, and the vines will wither; evil injures the lung. In the category of Overflowing Abundance, this is called 'sealing and storing.' Cold governs the transformation of things; heaven and earth are stern and frozen; the governance of storage spreads abroad, and the command of growth fails to be raised. Its transformation is bitter cold; its qi is hard; its governance is quiet stillness; its command is flowing and pouring; its pathological movement is drifting discharge, drenching, and welling up; its virtue manifests as congealing bleakness and cold mist; its calamitous change is ice, snow, frost, and hail. Its grains are beans and millet; its domestic animals are the pig and ox; its fruits are the chestnut and jujube; its colors are black, vermilion, and yellow; its flavors are salty, bitter, and sweet; its season-symbol is winter; its channels are the foot shaoyin and taiyang; its viscera are the kidney and heart; its creatures are the scaled and bare-skinned kinds; its substances are moist and full; its disease is distension. When the ascending yu prevails, the growth qi of fire fails to transform. When its governance goes to excess, the transforming qi of earth rises up greatly and dust-laden haze fills the exchange of qi; heavy rains fall in season, and evil injures the kidney. Hence it is said: when a phase fails to maintain its constant virtue, the one it overcomes comes to take revenge; when its governance constantly upholds its proper order, the one it overcomes is transformed in accordance with it. This is what is meant.

Huangdi said: Heaven is insufficient in the northwest, where the left is cold and the right is cool; earth is not full in the southeast, where the right is hot and the left is warm. What is the reason for this? Qibo said: This is a matter of yin and yang qi, of the principle of high and low, and of the difference between the great and the lesser. The southeast is yang, and yang's essence descends downward, so the right is hot and the left is warm there. The northwest is yin, and yin's essence is offered upward, so the left is cold and the right is cool there. Thus the land has its heights and depths, and the qi has its warmth and coolness: where the land is high, the qi is cold; where the land is low, the qi is hot. Therefore those who dwell amid cold and coolness develop distension, and those who dwell amid warmth and heat develop sores; purging relieves the distension, and inducing sweat relieves the sores. This is the constant principle of the opening and closing of the striae and pores, and merely reflects the difference between the great and the lesser degrees of yin and yang. Huangdi said: How does this bear upon longevity and early death? Qibo said: Where yin essence is offered upward, people live long; where yang essence descends, people die young. Huangdi said: Good. When people there fall ill, how should they be treated? Qibo said: For the qi of the northwest, disperse it and cool it; for the qi of the southeast, gather it in and warm it. This is what is meant by 'the same disease treated differently.' Hence it is said: where the qi is cold or cool, treat with cold and cooling methods, applying water to soak the body; where the qi is warm or hot, treat with warming and heating methods, strengthening what is guarded within. One must match the treatment to the local qi, and balance can thereby be achieved. In false, counterfeit presentations, do the reverse.

Huangdi said: Good. Within a single region, the qi's engendering and transformation, and people's longevity and early death, differ — what is the reason? Qibo said: This follows the principle of high and low; it is caused by the lay of the land. Where the land is lofty, yin qi governs it; where the land is low-lying, yang qi governs it. Where yang prevails, things mature before their allotted time; where yin prevails, things mature after their time. This is the constant principle of terrestrial pattern, and the way of engendering and transformation. Huangdi said: Does this also govern longevity and early death? Qibo said: Where the land is high, its qi favors long life; where the land is low, its qi favors early death — and this varies with the size of the land, small differences in elevation producing small differences, great differences producing great differences. Therefore one who treats disease must clearly understand the way of heaven and the pattern of earth, the alternating prevalence of yin and yang, the precedence and sequence of qi, the longevity and early death of people, and the timing of engendering and transformation — only then can one truly know a person's bodily form and qi.

Huangdi said: Good. In certain years, people do not fall ill, yet the qi of a viscus fails to respond or to function as expected — why is this? Qibo said: Heaven's qi controls it; the viscus qi has something it follows. Huangdi said: I wish to hear this in full. Qibo said: When shaoyang governs heaven, fire qi descends and presides below, and lung qi rises to follow it; whiteness arises and metal takes effect, while grasses and trees suffer calamity. Fire appears and scorches, subduing and consuming metal; great heat then prevails, causing coughing, sneezing, nasal congestion with bleeding, and stuffed nose; there are sores, alternating cold and heat, and swelling of the flesh. Wind moves over the earth, dust and sand fly up; there is heart pain and stomach-duct pain, reversal-counterflow, and blockage of the diaphragm. Its onset is sudden and rapid. When yangming governs heaven, dryness qi descends and presides below, and liver qi rises to follow it; blue-green arises and the function of wood is established, while earth suffers calamity. Bleak chill arrives repeatedly, wood is felled and grasses wither; there is rib-side pain, reddened eyes, trembling and shivering with chattering teeth, and sinew wilting that prevents long standing. Sudden heat arrives, earth then grows hot; yang qi is pent up and bursts forth as pain; urination changes; there is alternating cold and heat resembling malarial disorder, and in severe cases heart pain. Fire spreads over dried grass, flowing water does not freeze, and hibernating creatures appear out of season. When taiyang governs heaven, cold qi descends and presides below, and heart qi rises to follow it, so that fire also grows bright; vermilion arises, and metal thereby suffers calamity. Cold and clear chill arise from time to time, and when they prevail, water freezes. Fire qi is lofty and bright: there is heat in the heart, vexation, dryness of the throat, frequent thirst, sneezing and nasal congestion, tendency to sorrow, and frequent yawning. When heat qi moves recklessly, cold then takes revenge: frost falls out of season, there is forgetfulness, and in severe cases heart pain. The soil becomes moist, water grows abundant, cold invades, and deep yin transforms into dampness; the qi changes and things become water and fluid retained internally — there is fullness in the middle and inability to eat, numbness of the skin and heaviness of the flesh, and impaired function of the sinews and vessels; in severe cases there is swelling of the instep and abscess on the back of the body. When jueyin governs heaven, wind qi descends and presides below, and spleen qi rises to follow it, so that earth also grows abundant; yellow arises, and water thereby suffers calamity. The function of earth is altered: there is heaviness of the body, wilting of the muscles and flesh, reduced appetite, and blandness in the mouth. Wind sweeps through the great void, clouds and objects sway and shake, the eyes spin and the ears ring. Fire indulges its violence, the earth then grows hot, great heat consumes and sears, red discharge flows downward, hibernating creatures appear repeatedly, flowing water does not freeze — its onset is swift and sudden. When shaoyin governs heaven, heat qi descends and presides below, and lung qi rises to follow it; whiteness arises and metal takes effect, while grasses and trees suffer calamity. There is panting, vomiting, alternating cold and heat, sneezing, nasal congestion with bleeding, and stuffed nose; great heat prevails widely, and in severe cases there are burning sores and ulcers, heat so fierce as to melt metal and liquefy stone. The earth then grows dry and clear, bleak chill arrives repeatedly; there is rib-side pain and frequent sighing, killing severity prevails, and grasses and trees are transformed. When taiyin governs heaven, damp qi descends and presides below, and kidney qi rises to follow it; blackness arises and water is transformed; dust-laden haze, clouds, and rain appear; there is discomfort in the chest, impotence, and the qi is greatly weakened so that it cannot rise and cannot function. At such times there is, on the contrary, pain in the lower back and buttocks, and difficulty in movement and turning. There is cold-limb reversal; the earth then stores yin, great cold is about to arrive, and hibernating creatures retreat early. There is oppressive pain below the heart; the earth cracks and ice hardens; there is pain in the lower abdomen that at times interferes with eating. When water overrides metal, the stored water increases, the flavor that dominates is then salty, and the flow of water diminishes.

Huangdi said: In certain years, pregnancies fail to come to term, and treatment does not reach completion — what qi causes this? Qibo said: Among the six qi and the five categories of creatures, there is mutual overcoming and control: those that share the same phase as the governing qi flourish, while those that differ decline. This is the way of heaven and earth, the constancy of engendering and transformation. Thus, when jueyin governs heaven, the furred creatures are quiescent, the feathered creatures thrive, and the shelled creatures fail to reach completion; when jueyin governs the earth below, the furred creatures thrive, the bare-skinned creatures are depleted, and the feathered creatures fail to reproduce. When shaoyin governs heaven, the feathered creatures are quiescent, the shelled creatures thrive, and the furred creatures fail to reach completion; when shaoyin governs the earth below, the feathered creatures thrive, and the shelled creatures are depleted and fail to reproduce. When taiyin governs heaven, the bare-skinned creatures are quiescent, the scaled creatures thrive, and the feathered creatures fail to reach completion; when taiyin governs the earth below, the bare-skinned creatures thrive, and the scaled creatures fail to reach completion. When shaoyang governs heaven, the feathered creatures are quiescent, the furred creatures thrive, and the bare-skinned creatures fail to reach completion; when shaoyang governs the earth below, the feathered creatures thrive, the shelled creatures are depleted, and the furred creatures fail to reproduce. When yangming governs heaven, the shelled creatures are quiescent, the feathered creatures thrive, and the shelled creatures fail to reach completion; when yangming governs the earth below, the shelled creatures thrive, the furred creatures are depleted, and the feathered creatures fail to reach completion. When taiyang governs heaven, the scaled creatures are quiescent and the bare-skinned creatures thrive; when taiyang governs the earth below, the scaled creatures are depleted and the bare-skinned creatures fail to reproduce. In every case, when a creature-category is overridden during the movement in which it fails to reach completion, its failure is severe. Thus the governing qi has that which it controls, and the established year has that which it generates; earth's qi controls what it overcomes, and heaven's qi controls what overcomes it. Heaven governs color, earth governs form; the decline and flourishing of the five categories each follow what is suitable to their qi. Hence there are years when pregnancies fail to come to term and treatment does not reach completion — this is the constancy of qi, what is called 'rooted within.' What is rooted without is likewise fivefold; thus the distinctions of engendering and transformation comprise five qi, five flavors, five colors, five categories, and five suitabilities. Huangdi said: What does this mean? Qibo said: What is rooted within is called the pivot of spirit — when spirit departs, the pivot ceases. What is rooted without is called the establishment of qi — when qi stops, transformation is severed. Thus each has its controls, each has its overcoming, each has its engendering, and each has its completion. Hence it is said: one who does not understand what influence the year adds, and the sameness or difference of qi, is not fit to speak of engendering and transformation. This is what is meant.

Huangdi said: When qi begins, engendering and transformation occur; when qi disperses, form comes into being; when qi spreads, multiplication and nurture occur; when qi ends, the outward image changes — in this, all things arrive at the same principle. And yet, in what the five flavors provide, engendering and transformation may be thin or thick, and maturation may be scant or abundant, so that the end and beginning differ — what is the reason for this? Qibo said: It is controlled by earth's qi. It is not that heaven fails to engender — it is that earth fails to foster growth uniformly. Huangdi said: I wish to hear this principle. Qibo said: Cold, heat, dryness, and dampness produce different transformations. Thus, when shaoyang governs the earth below, cold-toxin fails to arise; its flavor is pungent; it is treated with bitter and sour; its grains are blue-green and vermilion. When yangming governs the earth below, damp-toxin fails to arise; its flavor is sour; its qi is damp; it is treated with pungent, bitter, and sweet; its grains are vermilion and white. When taiyang governs the earth below, heat-toxin fails to arise; its flavor is bitter; it is treated with bland and salty; its grains are yellow and black. When jueyin governs the earth below, clear-cold-toxin fails to arise; its flavor is sweet; it is treated with sour and bitter; its grains are blue-green and red; its qi is concentrated and its flavor is pure. When shaoyin governs the earth below, cold-toxin fails to arise; its flavor is pungent; it is treated with pungent, bitter, and sweet; its grains are white and vermilion. When taiyin governs the earth below, dryness-toxin fails to arise; its flavor is salty; its qi is hot; it is treated with sweet and salty; its grains are yellow and black. When transformation is pure, the salty flavor holds firm; when qi is concentrated, the pungent flavor transforms and both are brought into balance.

Hence it is said: in supplementing the upper or lower body, follow along with the qi; in treating the upper or lower, go against the qi — regulate according to the location and the prevalence or decline of cold and heat. Hence it is said: take the treatment from above or from below, from within or from without, in order to seek out the fault. Those who can tolerate toxicity are treated with potent medicines; those who cannot withstand toxicity are treated with mild medicines. This is what is meant. When the qi is contrary to the location of disease: if the disease is above, treat from below; if the disease is below, treat from above; if the disease is in the middle, treat from the sides. Treat heat with cold, but administer it warm; treat cold with heat, but administer it cool; treat warmth with clearing coolness, but administer it cold; treat clear coolness with warmth, but administer it hot. Thus, whether dissolving or paring away, inducing vomiting or purging, supplementing or draining, the same method applies whether the disease is chronic or recent. Huangdi said: When disease is in the middle, yet is neither excess nor hard, now gathering and now dispersing, what then? Qibo said: How thorough is this question! Where there is no fixed accumulation, seek out the affected viscus; if it is deficient, supplement it — expel the pathogen with medicine, follow up with diet, and apply water-soaking therapy; harmonize the interior and exterior, and the disease can be brought to complete resolution. Huangdi said: For medicines with toxicity and without toxicity, is there a rule for their administration? Qibo said: Disease may be chronic or recent, prescriptions may be large or small, and medicines may be toxic or non-toxic — there is indeed a constant rule to be observed. When treating disease with highly toxic medicine, stop when six-tenths of the pathogen is removed; with ordinarily toxic medicine, stop at seven-tenths removed; with mildly toxic medicine, stop at eight-tenths removed; with non-toxic medicine, stop at nine-tenths removed. Thereafter, grains, meats, fruits, and vegetables should be used to nourish and complete the recovery — do not overdo it, lest one injure the correct qi. If the disease is not yet eliminated, resume treatment according to the same method. One must always take the year's qi into account first, and never assault the harmony of heaven; do not further fill what is already full, do not further deplete what is already deficient, thereby bringing calamity upon a person from heaven itself; do not induce evil qi, and do not lose the correct qi — these errors would cut short a person's long life. Huangdi said: In those with chronic illness, whose qi, though following treatment, does not regain health, and who remain emaciated even after the disease has left — what then? Qibo said: How illuminating is this question of the sage! Transformation cannot be replaced by artificial means, and the proper season cannot be defied. Once the channels and network vessels are opened and the blood and qi made compliant, restore what is insufficient until it is equal with the rest of the body; nourish it and harmonize it, remain still and await the proper time, carefully guard the qi and do not let it be displaced — then the bodily form will become manifest and the qi of life will grow and flourish. This is called the way of the sage-king. Hence the Great Essentials say: do not replace transformation, do not defy the season; one must nourish, one must harmonize, and await its natural return. This is what is meant. Huangdi said: Good.

素问.title_en: Comprehensive Discourse on the Correct Chronicle of the Six Origins
paragraphs_en: The Yellow Emperor asked: I already understand the six transformations and six changes of the qi, the ascendancy and revenge [between the five phases] and their excessive governance, and the proper order of the flavors — sweet, bitter, acrid, salty, sour, and bland. But as for the transformations of the five movements — how at times they follow the five qi and at times oppose them, how at times they follow the qi of heaven while opposing the qi of earth, or follow the qi of earth while opposing the qi of heaven, how at times they are mutually accordant and at times discordant — these matters I have not yet been able to grasp clearly. I wish to comprehend the order of heaven and follow the principles of earth, to harmonize the movements and regulate their transformations, so that upper and lower may unite in virtue without usurping one another's proper sequence, so that the rising and falling of heaven and earth do not lose their fitness, so that the five movements proceed and unfold without deviating from their governance, and so that the proper flavors may be regulated accordingly — how is this accordance and opposition to be understood? Qibo bowed his head twice and replied: How illuminating is this question! This is the guiding thread of heaven and earth, the very wellspring of transformation and change — who but a sage sovereign could fathom its ultimate principle? Though I am unworthy, allow me to set forth this Way, that it may never be extinguished and remain unaltered through the ages. The Emperor said: I wish master to arrange these matters in sequence, to sort them by category and divide their governing domains, to distinguish their respective offices, to clarify the numbers of their qi, and to make plain their correct transformations — may I hear of this? Qibo said: One must first establish the [stem-branch designation of the] year in order to clarify its qi — the numbered sequence of the movements of metal, wood, water, fire, and earth, and the transformations wrought by cold, heat, dryness, dampness, wind, and fire as they preside and govern. Then the Way of heaven becomes visible, the qi of the people can be regulated, the unfolding and contracting of yin and yang draw near [to understanding] without confusion, and what can be enumerated can indeed be enumerated. Allow me now to set this forth in full.
The Emperor asked: What is the governance of greater yang [as controller of heaven]? Qibo replied: This is the chronicle of the chen and xu years [the dragon and dog earthly branches].

Greater yang governs heaven, with great wood as the central movement and greater yin governing earth, in the renchen and renxu years (ren being a yang wood heavenly stem, chen the dragon and xu the dog earthly branches). Its movement is wind; its transformation manifests as resounding turbulence, as things bursting open and splitting forth; its pathological excess manifests as violent shaking, uprooting, snapping, and toppling; its disease manifests as dizziness, tremor, and dimness of vision.
Great wood, lesser fire, great earth, lesser metal, great water.

Greater yang governs heaven, with great fire as the central movement and greater yin governing earth, in the wuchen and wuxu years (wu being a yang fire heavenly stem), which are of the same [influence] as an unmodified great-fire year. Its movement is heat; its transformation manifests as warmth, summer-heat, and stifling sultriness; its pathological excess manifests as blazing conflagration and seething ebullition; its disease manifests as heat stagnation.
Great fire, lesser earth, great metal, lesser water, lesser wood.

Greater yang governs heaven, with great earth as the central movement and greater yin governing earth, in the jiachen and jiaxu years (jia being a yang wood stem), both of which are 'year-conjunction' years [suihui, where the stem-governed movement matches the branch-governed qi of the year]. Its movement is yin turbidity and dust-laden mist; its transformation manifests as softening moisture and heavy, saturating dampness; its pathological excess manifests as startling tremors and sudden squalls; its disease manifests as dampness and heaviness in the lower body.
Great earth, lesser metal, great water, great wood, lesser fire.

Greater yang governs heaven, with great metal as the central movement and greater yin governing earth, in the gengchen and gengxu years (geng being a yang metal stem). Its movement is coolness; its transformation manifests as fog, dew, and the soughing of the autumn wind; its pathological excess manifests as stern killing and withering decay; its disease manifests as dryness, oppression of the back, dizziness, and fullness in the chest.
Great metal, lesser water, lesser wood, great fire, lesser earth.

Greater yang governs heaven, with great water as the central movement and greater yin governing earth, in the bingchen and bingxu years (bing being a yang fire stem), both being 'heaven-conjunction' years [tianfu, where the stem-governed movement matches the qi governing heaven]. Its movement is cold; its transformation manifests as congealing, bleak, biting chill; its pathological excess manifests as ice, snow, frost, and hail; its disease manifests as severe cold lodging in the valleys [of the muscles, i.e., the interstices between the large muscle masses].
Great water, great wood, lesser fire, great earth, lesser metal.

In all years when greater yang governs heaven in this manner, the transformation and movement of qi run ahead of the heavenly [calendrical] schedule. The qi of heaven is stern and the qi of earth is still; cold descends upon the great void and the yang qi cannot exercise its command; water and earth unite in virtue, corresponding above to the stars Chenxing [Mercury] and Zhenxing [Saturn]. Its grains are the black and the yellow; its governance is stern; its ordinances are unhurried. When the governance of cold is greatly asserted, the marshes show no trace of the fiery blaze [of summer], so that fire, when it does arise, must await its proper time. Lesser yang governs from within, so that seasonal rains reach their limit; when the rain ceases at its extreme it reverts to greater yin, clouds gather toward the northern pole, and the transformation of dampness spreads abroad, its moisture flowing to nourish the myriad things; cold spreads above while thunder stirs below, and the qi of cold and dampness contend within the qi-intersection [the meeting zone between the qi of heaven and earth]. The people suffer illness from cold and dampness, with wasting of the flesh, flaccidity and non-function of the legs, diarrhea with undigested food, and blood overflow. In the first [seasonal] qi, as the qi of earth shifts, the qi becomes greatly warm and plants flourish early; the people suffer pestilential illness, warm-disease arises, with fever, headache, vomiting, and sores in the flesh and interstices. In the second qi, unseasonable great coolness arrives instead; the people become subdued, plants encounter cold, and fire qi is thereby suppressed; the people suffer from stagnation of qi and fullness in the middle, and cold begins to set in. In the third qi, the governance of heaven is displayed, cold qi circulates, and rain descends; the people suffer from cold that instead produces heat in the interior, with welling-abscesses and flushing discharge, heat in the heart, dizziness and oppression — those not treated will die. In the fourth qi, wind and dampness contend with one another; wind transforms into rain, so that growth, transformation, and maturation proceed; the people suffer from great heat, shortness of qi, wasting of the flesh, flaccidity of the legs, and discharge of red and white [matter, i.e., dysenteric flux]. In the fifth qi, yang returns to transform, plants grow further, and transformation and maturation proceed; the people become at ease. In the final qi, the qi of earth is rectified, the ordinance of dampness circulates, yin congeals in the great void, dust-laden mist darkens the countryside, and the people become sorrowful and bleak; cold winds arrive, and where this is contrary [i.e. where warmth intrudes], pregnancies will miscarry and die. Therefore in such years one should use the bitter flavor to dry and to warm; one must break the stagnant qi, first replenishing its source of transformation, restraining the movement-qi [that is excessive] and supporting that which it overcomes, so as not to allow sudden excess to generate disease. Eat the grain proper to the year to preserve one's true qi, and avoid vacuity-evils so as to secure one's true [qi]. Adjust according to whether the qi is concordant or discordant [with the season], regulating by more or less: where cold and dampness coincide, use drying and heating transformation; where they diverge, use drying and dampening transformation — thus where they coincide, use more [of the corrective], where they diverge, use less. When employing cold [remedies], avoid [administering them in] cold [seasons]; when employing cool remedies, avoid cool seasons; when employing warm remedies, avoid warm seasons; when employing hot remedies, avoid hot seasons — diet should follow the same rule. Where there is a false [semblance contrary to the true pattern], one may act contrary to the norm; to act contrary to this [principle] causes disease — this is what is meant by 'according to the season.'
The Emperor said: Good. What is the governance of yang brightness [as controller of heaven]? Qibo replied: This is the chronicle of the mao and you years [the rabbit and rooster earthly branches].

Yang brightness governs heaven, with lesser wood as the central movement and lesser yin governing earth; coolness and heat, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern [in these years], equivalent to an unmodified great-metal year. In the dingmao year — a year-conjunction year — and the dingyou year, the movement progresses from wind to coolness to heat.
Lesser wood, great fire, lesser earth, great metal, lesser water.

Yang brightness governs heaven, with lesser fire as the central movement and lesser yin governing earth; cold and rain, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern, equivalent to an unmodified great-metal year. In the guimao and guiyou years, the movement progresses from heat to cold to rain.
Lesser fire, great earth, lesser metal, great water, great wood.

Yang brightness governs heaven, with lesser earth as the central movement and lesser yin governing earth; wind and coolness, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern. In the jimao and jiyou years, the movement progresses from rain to wind to coolness.
Lesser earth, great metal, lesser water, lesser wood, great fire.

Yang brightness governs heaven, with lesser metal as the central movement and lesser yin governing earth; heat and cold, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern, equivalent to an unmodified great-metal year. The yimao year is a heaven-conjunction year, and the yiyou year is both a year-conjunction and a supreme-unity heaven-conjunction year; the movement progresses from coolness to heat to cold.
Lesser metal, great water, great wood, lesser fire, great earth.

Yang brightness governs heaven, with lesser water as the central movement and lesser yin governing earth; rain and wind, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern — in the xinmao year this accords with a lesser-earth [movement]. In the xinyou and xinmao years, the movement progresses from cold to rain to wind.
Lesser water, lesser wood, great fire, great earth, great metal.

In all years when yang brightness governs heaven in this manner, the transformation and movement of qi run behind the heavenly schedule. The qi of heaven is urgent and the qi of earth is bright; yang exclusively exercises command, and blazing summer-heat prevails widely, so that things become dry and hardened, while a pure wind governs. Wind and dryness circulate crosswise, flowing through the qi-intersection; yang is abundant and yin scant; clouds hasten toward the chambers of rain, and the transformation of dampness thereby spreads. When dryness reaches its extreme, moisture follows. Its grains are the white and the red; among the supplementary grains, that governed by the greater [movement dominates]; its depleted creatures are the white-shelled and the feathered species. Metal and fire unite in virtue, corresponding above to the stars Taibai [Venus] and Yinghuo [Mars]. Its governance is sharp and its ordinances sudden; hibernating creatures appear, and flowing water does not freeze. The people suffer cough, obstruction of the throat, alternating cold and heat, sudden shivering, chill, and urinary blockage. When coolness precedes and is forceful, the furred creatures perish; when heat follows and is sudden, the shelled creatures suffer calamity. Its outbreaks of restless ascendancy-and-revenge cause turmoil and great disorder; the qi of coolness and heat contend within the qi-intersection. In the first qi, as the qi of earth shifts, yin begins to congeal and the qi begins to turn stern; water freezes and cold rain forms in transformation; the people suffer from heat in the middle with swelling, puffy swelling of the face and eyes, excessive sleepiness, nasal obstruction, nosebleed, sneezing, yawning, and vomiting, with yellow-red urine, and in severe cases painful urinary dribbling. In the second qi, yang spreads abroad and the people become at ease; things grow and flourish, yet pestilence arrives in force and the people are prone to sudden death. In the third qi, the governance of heaven is displayed, coolness circulates, dryness and heat intermingle, and when dryness reaches its extreme moisture follows; the people suffer alternating cold and heat. In the fourth qi, cold rain descends; illness manifests as sudden collapse, shivering and delirious raving, shortness of qi, dry throat with craving for drink, as well as heart pain, welling-abscess swelling, sores, malarial cold disorders, wasting of the bones, and bloody stool. In the fifth qi, the ordinance of spring is reversed and prevails; plants grow and flourish, and the people's qi is harmonious. In the final qi, yang qi spreads abroad, the season turns unseasonably warm, hibernating creatures emerge, and flowing water does not freeze; the people are healthy and at peace, though warm disease may occur. Therefore eat the grain proper to the year to settle the qi, and eat the supplementary grains to dispel evil qi. In such years one should use salty, bitter, and acrid flavors, to sweat, to clear, and to disperse, so as to settle the movement-qi and prevent the reception of evil, to break stagnant qi, and to replenish the source of transformation. Regulate according to the lightness or severity of cold and heat, using more or less accordingly: where heat coincides [with the heavenly qi], favor transformation governed by heaven; where coolness coincides [with the earthly qi], favor transformation governed by earth. When employing cool remedies avoid cool seasons; when employing hot remedies avoid hot seasons; when employing cold remedies avoid cold seasons; when employing warm remedies avoid warm seasons — diet should follow the same rule. Where there is a false [semblance], act contrary to this — such is the Way. To act contrary to this principle throws into disorder the constant thread of heaven and earth, and disturbs the chronicle of yin and yang.
The Emperor said: Good. What is the governance of lesser yang [as controller of heaven]? Qibo replied: This is the chronicle of the yin and shen years [the tiger and monkey earthly branches].

Lesser yang governs heaven, with great wood as the central movement and reverting yin governing earth, in the renyin and renshen years. Its movement is wind that stirs and drums; its transformation manifests as resounding turbulence, things bursting open and splitting forth; its pathological excess manifests as violent shaking, uprooting, snapping, and toppling; its disease manifests as tremor, dizziness, pain in the limbs and ribsides, and fright.
Great wood, lesser fire, great earth, lesser metal, great water.

Lesser yang governs heaven, with great fire as the central movement and reverting yin governing earth, in the wuyin and wushen years, both heaven-conjunction years. Its movement is summer-heat; its transformation manifests as warmth, clamor, and stifling sultriness; its pathological excess manifests as blazing conflagration and seething ebullition; its disease manifests as heat stagnating above, overflow and discharge of blood, and heart pain.
Great fire, lesser earth, great metal, lesser water, lesser wood.

Lesser yang governs heaven, with great earth as the central movement and reverting yin governing earth, in the jiayin and jiashen years. Its movement is overcast rain; its transformation manifests as softening moisture and heavy, saturating dampness; its pathological excess manifests as startling tremors and sudden squalls; its disease manifests as heaviness of the body, puffy swelling, focal distention, and fluid retention.
Great earth, lesser metal, great water, great wood, lesser fire.

Lesser yang governs heaven, with great metal as the central movement and reverting yin governing earth, in the gengyin and gengshen years, equivalent to an unmodified great-metal year. Its movement is coolness; its transformation manifests as fog, dew, and clear, cutting [air]; its pathological excess manifests as stern killing and withering decay; its disease manifests [as disorders of] the shoulders, back, and chest.
Great metal, lesser water, lesser wood, great fire, lesser earth.

Lesser yang governs heaven, with great water as the central movement and reverting yin governing earth, in the bingyin and bingshen years. Its movement is cold severity; its transformation manifests as congealing, bleak, biting chill; its pathological excess manifests as ice, snow, frost, and hail; its disease manifests as cold with puffy swelling.
Great water, great wood, lesser fire, great earth, lesser metal.

In all years when lesser yang governs heaven in this manner, the transformation and movement of qi run ahead of the heavenly schedule. The qi of heaven is upright while the qi of earth is turbulent; wind arises suddenly and violently, bending trees and driving sand aloft; blazing fire then flows forth. Yin proceeds while yang transforms, and seasonal rains respond in due time; fire and wood unite in virtue, corresponding above to the stars Yinghuo [Mars] and Suixing [Jupiter]. Its grains are the red and the blue-green; its governance is severe and its ordinances turbulent. Thus wind and heat spread together, clouds and vapors seethe, greater yin [dampness] flows crosswise, cold arrives from time to time, and coolness and rain arise together. The people suffer cold in the interior, with sores erupting externally and diarrhea and fullness internally; the sage, meeting with this, harmonizes with it rather than contending against it. As [wind and cold] alternately advance and retreat, the people suffer alternating cold and heat, malarial disorders, diarrhea, deafness, blurred vision, vomiting, and swelling and discoloration above, with a sense of oppression. In the first qi, as the qi of earth shifts, wind prevails and causes shaking; cold then departs, the season turns greatly warm, and plants and trees flourish early. Cold that arrives does not kill, and warm disease then arises; the disease manifests as oppressed qi rising above, overflow of blood, redness of the eyes, coughing counterflow, headache, [nose]bleeding, fullness of the ribsides, and sores in the skin and interstices. In the second qi, fire is instead suppressed, white dust rises on all sides, clouds hasten toward the chambers of rain, wind cannot overcome dampness, and rain falls; the people are at ease. The disease manifests as heat stagnating above, coughing counterflow, vomiting, sores erupting within, discomfort of the chest and throat, headache, fever, clouded confusion, and purulent sores. In the third qi, the governance of heaven is displayed, blazing summer-heat arrives, lesser yang presides above, and rain reaches its limit. The people suffer heat in the interior, deafness, blurred vision, overflow of blood, purulent sores, coughing and vomiting, nasal obstruction, nosebleed, thirst, sneezing, yawning, painful throat obstruction, red eyes, and are prone to sudden death. In the fourth qi, coolness arrives, blazing summer-heat is interspersed in its transformation, white dew descends, and the people's qi is harmonious and at peace, though the disease may manifest as fullness and heaviness of the body. In the fifth qi, yang then departs and cold arrives; rain descends, the pores [qi-gates] close, hardy trees wither early; the people avoid the evil of cold, and the noble person keeps himself well guarded. In the final qi, the qi of earth is rectified, wind arrives, the myriad things paradoxically grow anew, and dense fog and mist prevail. The disease manifests as inability to control the joints and orifices, heart pain, and coughing from failure of yang qi to be stored. One must restrain the excessive movement-qi and assist that which it overcomes; one must break the stagnant qi, first drawing upon the source of transformation, so that sudden excess does not arise and severe disease does not develop. Therefore in such years salty and acrid flavors are suitable, as is the sour; use draining and discharging, soaking and dispersing methods, observing whether the qi is cold or warm in order to regulate its excess. Where wind and heat coincide, favor cold-natured transformation more; where they diverge, favor it less. When employing hot remedies avoid hot seasons; when employing warm remedies avoid warm seasons; when employing cold remedies avoid cold seasons; when employing cool remedies avoid cool seasons — diet should follow the same rule. Such is the Way. Where there is a false [semblance], act contrary to this; to act contrary to this principle is the very stairway to disease.
The Emperor said: Good. What is the governance of greater yin [as controller of heaven]? Qibo replied: This is the chronicle of the chou and wei years [the ox and sheep earthly branches].

Greater yin governs heaven, with lesser wood as the central movement and greater yang governing earth; coolness and heat, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern, equivalent to an unmodified great-earth year. In the dingchou and dingwei years, the movement progresses from wind to coolness to heat.
Lesser wood, great fire, lesser earth, great metal, lesser water.

Greater yin governs heaven, with lesser fire as the central movement and greater yang governing earth; cold and rain, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern. In the guichou and guiwei years, the movement progresses from heat to cold to rain.
Lesser fire, great earth, lesser metal, great water, great wood.

Greater yin governs heaven, with lesser earth as the central movement and greater yang governing earth; wind and coolness, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern, equivalent to an unmodified great-earth year. Both the jichou and jiwei years are supreme-unity heaven-conjunction years; the movement progresses from rain to wind to coolness.
Lesser earth, great metal, lesser water, lesser wood, great fire.

Greater yin governs heaven, with lesser metal as the central movement and greater yang governing earth; heat and cold, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern. In the yichou and yiwei years, the movement progresses from coolness to heat to cold.
Lesser metal, great water, great wood, lesser fire, great earth.

Greater yin governs heaven, with lesser water as the central movement and greater yang governing earth; rain and wind, ascendancy and revenge, follow the same pattern, equivalent to an unmodified great-earth year. In the xinchou and xinwei years, the movement progresses from cold to rain to wind.
Lesser water, lesser wood, great fire, lesser earth, great metal.

In all years when greater yin governs heaven in this manner, the transformation and movement of qi run behind the heavenly schedule. Yin exclusively exercises its governance and yang qi retreats and withdraws; great winds arise from time to time, the qi of heaven descends and the qi of earth rises, the open plains grow dark with mist, white dust rises on every side, clouds race toward the southern pole, cold rain arrives repeatedly, and things reach maturity only belatedly in summer. The people suffer from cold and dampness, with abdominal fullness, distention and puffy swelling of the body, focal distention and counterflow, cold reversal, and contracture; dampness and cold unite in virtue, and yellow-black dust darkens [the sky], circulating through the qi-intersection, corresponding above to the stars Zhenxing [Saturn] and Chenxing [Mercury]. Its governance is stern and its ordinances silent; its grains are the yellow and the black. Thus yin congeals above while cold accumulates below; when cold water overcomes fire, ice and hail form; when the light of yang fails to govern, killing qi then circulates. Thus where there is excess, high ground is favorable; where there is insufficiency, low ground is favorable; where there is excess, [planting and harvest is] appropriately late; where there is insufficiency, appropriately early — this is the benefit of earth and the transformation of qi, which the people's qi also follows; among the supplementary grains, that governed by the greater [movement] should be designated. In the first qi, as the qi of earth shifts, cold departs, the qi of spring is rectified, wind arrives, growth spreads over the myriad things bringing them to flourish, and the people's qi is at ease and unfolds freely; wind and dampness contend with one another, and rain therefore comes late. The people suffer overflow of blood, tightness and stiffness of the sinews and vessels, disfunction of the joints, heaviness of the body, and flaccidity of the sinews. In the second qi, the great fire [of summer] is rectified, things receive its transforming influence, and the people are harmonious; yet pestilential warm disease spreads widely, afflicting near and far alike; dampness and steam contend with one another, and rain falls at its proper season. In the third qi, the governance of heaven is displayed, dampness qi descends while the qi of earth rises, rain falls at its proper season, and cold follows in its wake. Those affected by cold and dampness suffer heaviness of the body, puffy swelling, and fullness of the chest and abdomen. In the fourth qi, the dreaded fire [of the interim] presides, sultry steaming transformation occurs, the qi of earth rises while the qi of heaven is blocked and obstructed; cold wind blows at dawn and dusk, steam and heat contend with one another, plants and trees are wrapped in condensed mist, and the transformation of dampness fails to flow freely; then white dew spreads its shade, bringing on the ordinance of autumn. The people suffer heat in the interstices, sudden overflow of blood, malarial disorders, fullness and heat of the heart and abdomen with distention, and in severe cases puffy swelling. In the fifth qi, the bleak ordinance [of autumn] has already taken hold, cold dew descends, frost falls early, plants and trees turn yellow and shed their leaves, and cold qi reaches the body; the noble person keeps himself well guarded, while the people suffer disorders of the skin and interstices. In the final qi, cold asserts itself greatly and dampness transforms greatly; frost accumulates, yin congeals, and water hardens into ice; the light of yang fails to govern. Those affected by cold suffer restriction and rigidity of the joints and pain of the lower back and buttocks; cold and dampness press upon the qi-intersection and give rise to illness. One must break the stagnant qi and draw upon the source of transformation, augmenting the qi of the year so as not to let evil qi prevail; eat the grain proper to the year to preserve one's true qi, and eat the supplementary grains to safeguard one's essence. Therefore in such years the bitter flavor should be used to dry and to warm; in severe cases, use methods of releasing and discharging. If one neither releases nor discharges, then damp qi will overflow externally, the flesh will fester and the skin split, with water and blood flowing together. One must assist the yang fire so as to withstand severe cold, judging by more or less according to whether the qi coincides or diverges: where cold coincides, use heat-natured transformation; where dampness coincides, use drying transformation; where they diverge, use less; where they coincide, use more. When employing cool remedies avoid cool seasons; when employing cold remedies avoid cold seasons; when employing warm remedies avoid warm seasons; when employing hot remedies avoid hot seasons — diet should follow the same rule. Where there is a false [semblance], act contrary to this — such is the Way. To act contrary to this principle causes disease.
The Emperor said: Good. What is the governance of lesser yin [as controller of heaven]? Qibo replied: This is the chronicle of the zi and wu years [the rat and horse earthly branches].

Lesser yin governs heaven, with great wood as the central movement and yang brightness governing earth, in the renzi and renwu years. Its movement is wind that stirs and drums; its transformation manifests as resounding turbulence, things bursting open and splitting forth; its pathological excess manifests as violent shaking, uprooting, snapping, and toppling; its disease manifests as fullness of the limbs.
Great wood, lesser fire, great earth, lesser metal, great water.

Lesser yin governs heaven, with great fire as the central movement and yang brightness governing earth. The wuzi year is a heaven-conjunction year, and the wuwu year is a supreme-unity heaven-conjunction year. Its movement is blazing summer-heat; its transformation manifests as warm brilliance and stifling sultriness; its pathological excess manifests as blazing conflagration and seething ebullition; its disease manifests as heat above with overflow of blood.
Great fire, lesser earth, great metal, lesser water, lesser wood.

Lesser yin governs heaven, with great earth as the central movement and yang brightness governing earth, in the jiazi and jiawu years. Its movement is overcast rain; its transformation manifests as softening moisture and timely rain; its pathological excess manifests as startling tremors and sudden squalls; its disease manifests as fullness in the middle and heaviness of the body.
Great earth, lesser metal, great water, great wood, lesser fire.

Lesser yin governs heaven, with great metal as the central movement and yang brightness governing earth, in the gengzi and gengwu years, equivalent to an unmodified great-metal year. Its movement is severe coolness; its transformation manifests as fog, dew, and the soughing of the autumn wind; its pathological excess manifests as stern killing and withering decay; its disease manifests as coldness below.
Great metal, lesser water, lesser wood, great fire, lesser earth.
